On April 12, 2019, the legislature reached its third deadline for bills to be heard. MNCDHH has good news to report; all of the bills that we took the lead on advanced to the next level. The DeafBlind Intervenor bill is on its way to final passage. The remaining 6 bills are in omnibus bills, which is a large bill that includes many different bills that were heard and passed out of a committee. These 6 bills are either in the House omnibus bill only, or the Senate omnibus bill only, or they are in both bills. All will be part of the conference committee and could still get passed so we are hopeful. The bills led by others, and MNCDHH supports, have similar status as MNCDHH’s bills. They have passed out of committee, either through the House, the Senate, or both, and will move on to the next step. Only one bill did not make it through, the hearing aid insurance bill. Next, we will share specific updates for each bill. The Commission’s request for an increase of $450,000 was included in both the House and the Senate bills. The additional funds would cover a government relations specialist, a policy analyst, studies and research, leadership training, and increased costs of accommodations. This bill will be discussed in the House and Senate Jobs, Energy and Utilities Conference Committee. The House created a special grant program for MDS for $100,000 to cover the costs of children birth to age three for the next four years. The Senate would not give the bill a hearing. We will work to convince the Senate to agree to include the grant in the final bill. This will not completely fix MDS’s problem, but will provide the school with enough money to cover the estimated costs of children birth through 36 months for several years. Once they have demonstrated their success, they will be in a stronger position to get into the regular payment system and can come back to the legislature in two to four years. First we have to get the Senate to agree to the House positions. This bill will be discussed in the House and Senate Education Policy and Finance Conference Committee. The House included $64,000 for 2020 and 2021 to study reestablishing a program. The Senate included $50,000 for one year. This will be discussed in the House and Senate Higher Education Conference Committee. MSAD got the request for their operating costs in the House, but they only got their base in the Senate. They got $13 million in the House for asset preservation, a safety corridor and improving mechanical systems and accessibility in the dormitories. MSA has been providing legislative updates in their monthly Superintendent’s Corner. Be sure to follow them The bill had its final passage in the House. We will advocate for its final passage in the Senate. We think this will happen soon. Thanks to Sally, Mike and Andrew Prouty for their advocacy. The bill is in the House Health and Human Services Omnibus Bill. It didn’t get a hearing in the Senate because they ran out of time. The Chair of the Senate Committee said she would agree with the House version. The bill will be discussed in the House and Senate Health and Human Services Conference Committee. This bill did not advance. We will try again in the future. This bill was included in the Senate, but not in the House. We believe this bill is very important and are working hard to make sure that it gets included in the final bill and that the conference committee supports the Senate position. We are working to address Minnesota Management and Budget’s concerns. It’s not OK that 56% of state employees with disabilities leave state government within a year. The state needs to create a more inclusive and welcoming environment for people with disabilities to contribute their talents and meet workforce needs. The study will be addressed in the House and Senate Government Policy and Finance State Government Conference Committee. VR got its request for full funding in the House at $8 million and it got $7 million in the Senate. The bill is in both the House and the Senate omnibus bills and will be heard in the House and Senate State Government Finance and Policy Conference Committee. Both the House and the Senate included their request for $211,000 to address interpreter shortages and to make policy changes that will allow them to provide more services and more equipment in the telecommunication distribution program. The bill was heard in the House and didn’t get a hearing in the Senate. It does not have a good chance of passage. It will be discussed in the State Government and Elections Conference Committee. The conference committees began their discussions on the House and Senate versions of bills after legislators returned from their spring break on April 23, 2019. The House and the Senate is busy appointing members of the Conference Committees. The Committees will have to agree on what will be in the final bills. Once the bills are passed from their Committees, they will go to the floor of the House and the Senate for final passage. Then they get sent to the Governor to be signed. The Governor can either sign the bills, or veto all or parts of the bills. Things are moving fast. We still have more work to do before session ends on May 20, 2019. If you have any questions, please contact Mary Hartnett at

Tax professionals. Many people are surprised and don't realize that in order to become a tax preparer, you are not required to obtain a license. Anyone could call themselves a tax preparer and help you fill out your taxes. However, if you pay a person to prepare your taxes, that individual should be registered with the IRS, the Internal Revenue Service. Once registered, they get a PTIN, a Personal Tax Identification Number. Once they have a PTIN, they can then sign their name and PTIN on your tax documents in order to show the IRS who prepared your taxes. If you pay for a tax preparer's services and they do not sign their name and PTIN, please don't pay that person. I do also want to tell you that some states, such as California, Connecticut, Iowa, Maryland, New York, and Oregon do have their own individual state requirements for tax preparers, paid preparers. There are two states that require an exam for paid tax preparers, Oregon and Maryland. Now, though, most states do not require a license for tax preparer services. I do encourage you to do some research and look for two credentials. These two credentials mean that a person may represent you to the IRS if the IRS flags your account. These two credentials are CPA, Certified Public Accountant, and EA, Enrolled Agent. Also tax lawyers. Individuals with those three credentials could represent you with the IRS. However, there are many other tax preparer professionals who are experienced, who are qualified, and can help you fill out your taxes. It's important to make sure they have PTIN that they can document on your forms. Kramer Wealth Managers encourage you to ask your financial professionals to be sure that they have the right credentials, such as licenses and required registrations. Don't be scared to ask them. Ask them questions to make sure that they are the right fit to provide the services that you need, that they can help with your financials, and with your situation. We have developed a list of different professionals who are fluent in ASL or who have experience working with Deaf clients through interpreters from all over the state. We want our clients to work with professionals who truly can help establish plans and support your financial goals. If you have any questions or want to talk more about this, please contact us. We are more than happy to discuss this with you.

Also I've applied to a bunch of jobs and none of them have emailed me. So I might have to apply to be a lifeguard again. text_english: MNCDHH is aware of a critical shortage of DHH teachers in Minnesota and we have collected information from other organizations to share this information about the shortage with you. Every two years, the State of Minnesota is required to share information about the current status of the teacher work force here in our state, which is called a "Teacher Supply and Demand Report." The report includes information from school districts about if they are able to hire qualified teachers for their students. This includes all teacher positions, not only teachers of the deaf and hard of hearing students or teachers in special education. The most important question the report tries to answer is, "Is there enough of a supply of teachers to meet the demand for them?" Unfortunately, no. There are currently 373 teachers in Minnesota who have a DHH license, and 72 with an Oral/Aural license, but we need more teachers to meet the needs of students who are D/deaf or hard of hearing. There is a serious shortage of teachers all over the United States. This includes teachers with deaf/hard of hearing licensure. We will focus on D/HH licensed teachers in Minnesota. The D/HH licensing includes teachers with D/HH license and teachers with Auditory/Oral license. From 2013-2017, Minnesota's school districts statewide have reported that hiring teachers with DHH licensure were either "difficult to fill" or they "could not fill" the vacancies. The 2019 Teacher Supply and Demand Report included a list of positions in the 2018-2019 school year that were not filled because of a lack of qualified applicants. D/deaf or hard of hearing teaching positions were at the top of this list. Adding to the concern is that a high number of Minnesota’s current DHH teachers are expected to retire over the next few years. The median age of Minnesota’s DHH teachers is 46 years old. A July 2018 Gallup poll indicated that the average age a teacher retires is about 58. They will need to be replaced as they retire. The number of teacher preparation programs in deaf education in the U.S. is going down. In 2007, there were 68 teacher prep programs for deaf education. In 2019, there are 57. We are lucky to have the University of Minnesota’s program, but even they are receiving fewer applicants and producing fewer graduates every year. This is part of a national trend. Between the academic years of 2007-08 and 2015-16, there was a 23% decline in the number of people completing teacher preparation programs. There is some good news though. This year , the University of Minnesota received an increase in applications to their program, probably because they secured federal funding for some students. This funding is called Project PACT and is good for 5 years. The University of Minnesota is currently in their 2nd year with the program. Since cost is often a barrier to becoming a teacher, there are many financial programs being offered, including Project PACT. Watch MNCDHH’s video, “Minnesota Needs Teachers!” to learn more about those programs. Minnesota must have high standards for our teachers. Our students who are deaf, deafblind, and hard of hearing deserve quality teachers who can teach them in their preferred language. Our teachers must know how to teach all of the school requirements . They must understand the importance of communication equity, and be able to advocate for their students. They must also understand the whole child approach and ensure that their students have access to social opportunities with their peers. We also need more teachers who mirror the students they teach, which includes representation of teachers of color and representation of teachers who are deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing. Erin Toninato, who is the representative for Minnesota Administrators of Special Education and serves on PELSB’s Auditory-Oral Advisory and D/HH Advisory Subcommittees, recently said, “We all want high quality teachers – but we need to keep our licenses attractive and attainable to potential teacher candidates.” There are three PELSB Advisory Subcommittee groups meeting to address these issues. One group is discussing licensure standards for those who teach American Sign Language as a world language in K-12 settings. The second group is focused on Auditory-Oral DHH licensure, for teachers who work with students who primarily use spoken English. The third group is discussing DHH licensure, for teachers who work with students who primarily use American Sign Language . They will be working hard over the next year to make recommendations to PELSB related to these licenses. If you are not sure what PELSB is, it is short for Professional Educator Licensing and Standards Board, which was created by the legislature to oversee and implement teacher licensing for the state of Minnesota. Special education and deaf education is a very small part of a very big job that PELSB must do. There is a national trend to generalize all special education licenses. So far, we have been successful in keeping specialized licensure in Minnesota so that students who are deaf, deafblind, and hard of hearing can have qualified teachers. And for you, have you ever thought about becoming a qualified DHH licensed teacher? Minnesota needs you. Please reach out to Becca Jackson to talk about your options. Her email is Here is information about applying for the University of Minnesota’s teacher preparation program. While the deadline has passed for 2019, applications for those interested in starting fall 2020 will be due on March 1, 2020. For questions about the University of Minnesota’s program, please contact Debbie Golos at

Hello Everyone. Welcome to this special Vlog. Regarding American Sign Language . I just want you to understand, the meaning why ASL is a good and beautiful language. Communicate with Deaf and Hard of Hearing Community, like me . Understand, not just deaf community...kids or adults who have Autism, non-verbal, some depend on ASL also. I just want to inform you that ASL is a beautiful language, deaf people don't accept or tolerate discriminations, not okay at all. Discrimination, or negative comments. If kids deaf or hard of hearing non-verbal children depend on ASL. Most people should understand and try best effort to provide access to communication. Schools tend to have IEP right? If IEP mentions ASL means follow the request period. I am not here to express anger, here to help you all in the community to understand concept of ASL American Sign Language. Beautiful language, many depend on that language to communicate access. If see hearing people who have been taking ASL Classes, in-person OR online, online now due to COVID-19 but in general since then...become an expert in ASL! They have reason to be motivated and learn. I repeat: beautiful language. Amazing! You cannot remove that language, deaf community wont accept removal of ASL Communication access. I'm sorry, but if for example: a deaf person/student enter classroom sit and wait, teacher hearing at mainstream school. If no interpreter how can he/she understand, how?? impossible. if a deaf students enter deaf school/campus, different story. Im speaking of public schools, a non-verbal student some depend on ASL, if no access to ASL how? I'm just trying to help you understand why ASL is beautiful language, we cannot tolerate or think it s okay, ignore communication access that is needed. Again, ASL is great! Not be afraid to learn. I've recently been doing ASL Vlogs , because people I know Families, Friends have motivation to learn and I'm happy to provide those Lessons when needed. Please understand my perspective as a deaf hard of hearing person. My expression reason that cannot be ignored. Don't be afraid to learn...more you learn, you'll will become successful. Thank you!

Hi everyone! Welcome to my channel. If you're new, my name is Rachel. This is my namesign . This is my ASL vlog. Hi everyone! Welcome to my channel! I know I haven't posted in a long time, but I decided since I'm always too busy and I know I've never actually posted every day, so I've decided to try to post my ASL vlog weekly. So I won't be every day anymore. I still might try to film myself every day, but I'll only post one video a week. I think I can do that. Editing, subtitles, and uploading all take a long time and are a lot of work. I just don't have time. So I'll only do that for one video each week. If I film myself the other days, it will just be for practice signing but I won't post them. Another reason is that I don't really have a lot to say every day. It's just not interesting. Anyway I'm going to tell you what's been happening recently. I think it's been a few weeks since I last posted. I'm sorry about that. I got busy and felt like I didn't have anything to say. It's already the new year, so happy New Year! It's 2020! I go back to school next week. Not next Monday, but the Monday after, I go back. I'll let you know about that. I might have to move. I live in the dorms at CSUN and two or three weeks ago...so my roommate is an international student, so she can't fly home. Our dorm is actually an apartment. It has two bedrooms and another common area. So my roommate and I share one of the bedrooms. Two other girls have the other bedroom. My roommate is the only one who stayed. She heard what sounded like water dripping in the other bedroom. She thought that was not good, so she called student housing. They came to look at it and discovered that the sprinklers for if there's a fire broke It was leaking, so they had to fix it. They inspected it and found that all four floors had a leak. So it's going to be a really big job to fix all of it. So now we can't use that room in our apartment. It's not fixed yet, so the other two girls can't live there. My roommate and I don't know if we can stay or if we have to move too. Our bedroom was not affected and doesn't need to be fixed but we don't know yet if we're going to be allowed to stay. Monday my roommate is going to call student housing and ask if we can stay because the leak wasn't in our room. I really hope we can stay. I really don't want to move. It's so much work and I fly back one day before classes start. I can't go back early because I have a training for my job. I'm a lifeguard for a pool so I'm certified. I have to take the class again every two years. If I don't take the class I'll lose my certification and won't be able to continue to work. It's a two day class that's over the day before I fly back, so I can't change my flight. I'm stuck. If I have to move, I'll arrive and move that day. I might be able to do some the next day because I only have one class on Tuesday and it's only an hour long, but it's still a lot of work and I really hope I don't have to. I'll let you know what happens. I'll try to post the vlog weekly, not daily. I hope you enjoyed this video. If you want to see more, subscribe and like this video. If you have feedback on my signing, let me know in the comments below. See you later. Bye!

text_english: Hello, welcome back. Today, I'm showing ASL signs -- well, two signs that are similar. What are they? BUSY and BUSINESS. Oh, and maybe a third: MOVIE. Did YOU notice the difference? BUSY BUSY The "B" handshape on a relaxed other hand here, fist or relaxed. BUSY You can add facial expression to show: Wow, really busy. Really busy, or maybe "Sorry, I can't, I'm busy." BUSY "Busy", for example, have plans, a lot of work, a lot going on... That. BUSY. "Business", like a company... BUSINESS. BUSINESS Again, the "B" handshape on the other arm, BUSINESS BUSINESS Another way: BUSINESS. BUSINESS Again, the "B" handshape. The movement's a little different. BUSINESS or BUSINESS The sign means the same thing. "Business" BUSINESS Or, one little tip: If you want to sign "not your business", or "none of your business" "N-Y-B" -- the sign "None of your business." or not your business. NYB not your business Again: BUSY versus BUSINESS. Both use similar handshape and location the movement is a little different. Plus, busy can use facial expression to show busy versus business. BUSY BUSINESS Another sign that's similar is MOVIE. The differences: The non-dominant hand is palm inward Your dominant hand isn't in a "B"; it's more like a relaxed "5". MOVIE. MOVIE MOVIE or FILM. Think of like, in the past, those films would use a projection with light up on a *screen* MOVIE. It's like your hand is the screen. MOVIE. Oh, and one last sign I want to add is true-biz. TRUE-BIZ It's an ASL sign. There's not really one exact English word. It's like "for real" or "seriously", "sure enough". That, in the asl sign true-biz. True-biz. For more related to that sign true-biz, I'll add some links in the description, so that you can learn more about that sign. TRUE-BIZ. Again, we have BUSINESS "Not your business" BUSY MOVIE MOVIE Okay? I hope that that helped clarify those signs! If you want more videos with similar signs clarifying the differences, look over here. Thanks for watching, and I'll see you soon. Bye!

text_english: Ever wished for captions during a VRS call? Well, guess what? There's a hack you can use! Google’s Live Caption feature on Chrome can caption anything on the web… including calls using Convo Link! Let me show you how to turn it on. Go ahead and open Google Chrome. At the top-right corner, click on Chrome to see the dropdown menu. Find and click on Preferences. In the Settings menu at the bottom, you'll find Advanced. Click on that. A dropdown menu will open. Click on Accessibility. A menu window will appear. At the top, you will see Live Caption. Go ahead and turn it on. That's done. Now here's what it looks like with Convo Link! Take a look. INTERPRETER: Ready to call? JACK: Yes, thank you. INTERPRETER: I'll go ahead and call. Google has been working on making their platforms and apps more accessible. Live Caption may be a useful tool to use with Convo Link. Important to remember: Live Caption is auto captioning provided by Google, and Convo is not responsible for its accuracy. It is Wednesday, January 15. Ready for news?
What is it? NAD YAP .

The NAD filed a lawsuit to ask U.S. President Donald J. Trump and the White House to immediately begin providing American Sign Language interpreters during television broadcasts of their COVID-19 press conferences and briefings to make them accessible to deaf and hard of hearing people. Joining the NAD as counsel for the case is an international law firm, Arnold & Porter, that is dedicated to pro bono litigation and equal access issues. Together, we are representing the plaintiffs in this lawsuit. The NAD is an organizational plaintiff along with five deaf people who are also plaintiffs: Carlton Strail, Graham Forsey, Debra Fleetwood, John Rivera Jr., and Corey Axelrod. The reason for this lawsuit is to make sure deaf and hard of hearing people get same access to important COVID information and health related news from the White House and the President as everyone else. Such information is not accessible to us, with nothing available in ASL. And this access should not just be captioning, which is sometimes delayed and not always accurate, but also in ASL. Since March, 2020, the NAD, the National Council on Disability, and several legislators from Congress sent letters to the White House asking for ASL interpreters, but there has been no response yet. All 50 state governors have provided interpreters, although some have been not been consistent while others have always provided interpreters. The White House has never provided an ASL interpreter for COVID-19 even though the law, Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, requires that the White House make its communications accessible to all including in ASL for deaf and hard of hearing people. More than 4.7 million Americans have been infected with coronavirus with more than 150,000 dead. Deaf and hard of hearing people are more at risk to being affected with the coronavirus because we do not get the same updates and information at the same time as everyone else. That is why this lawsuit was filed, to make sure the highest levels of government provide ASL access for health information to deaf and hard of hearing people. We will bring further updates when there is progress in this case.

text_english: Hello! My name is Kevin I wanted to share an ASL testimony with you all Six years ago, my friend invited me to go to a prayer meeting at a church that had a 24 hour prayer room that you could access any time It was nice! It had chairs set up around the room There were pictures on the wall of various missionary families you could pray for There was also a large map on the wall so that you could pray for the Nations As my friend and I walked in, we each went our separate ways to have our own prayer time I went to the back and knelt down to pray I continued to pray and praise Him saying, "Jesus I love you", etc. But as I continued praying, I felt my energy building as I searched for a more complete expression of my worship I felt like my whole being, spirit and body, in wholeness and integration wanting to burst forth with praise So, I switched over to signing, "Jesus I love you, You are beautiful, You are King, You are Holy, I look to you!" I began to pray, "You are my Comforter, You comfort me" I thought, "How do I sign that?" "What does that mean exactly? Could I sign, 'You comfortable me...' No that doesn't work" I felt stuck and at a loss for words How would you sign the verb "to comfort"? What does that look like? As I continued to ponder these questions, a sign flashed across the canvas of my mind It was a new sign, one I had never seen At first, I did not understand what it meant When I tried to sign it, the revelation of the meaning struck me. "That's how you comfort me!" The sign is formed by circling the handshape meaning "I love you" over your heart Jesus speaks directly to your heart, reminding you of His love by saying over and over again, "I love you, I love you, I love you" This means that no matter what happens on your life journey, including the obstacles, frustrations, struggles, suffering, chaos In the midst of feeling overwhelmed, Jesus is always speaking the reminder of His love for you directly to your heart He will always comfort you with His love In those moments I feel His peace and remind myself that His love is true. I look to Him and place my trust in Him I feel safe I know that I can continue on my journey in rest and peace, placing my full faith and trust in Him Even though ASL is not my heart language I deeply cherish ASL and feel a strong connection to it God knows that we can commune through ASL which is something very special to me And for the Deaf? Well, this means that Jesus knows your heart language! He knows sign language! He is ready and excited to commune with you, sharing so much with you directly through His Holy Spirit In the same way you would sit down to share a meal with a friend, conversing and sharing your heart with each other Jesus is ready and excited to commune with you I hope you will share your stories, faith journeys, and testimonies with us or with me. I'm looking forward to it! Thank you for watching, Much love!

text_english: Hello, my name is LT. I am the Acting VP for the Deaf Centers of Nevada Board. The Board and the Executive Leadership Team want to share some updates with the community regarding DCN. As you may have heard, the DCN Board has voted to give back the funding in the best interest of the organization. Therefore, services will no longer be provided by DCN as of December 30, 2020. Please keep in mind that DCN will continue to provide services until December 30, 2020. Services include advocacy, equipment, youth and family services, and career services. Contact our front desk to schedule an appointment with one of our advocates. Contact information will be shown at the end of this video. The DCN Board is meeting with the Aging and Disability Services Division on a weekly basis to discuss transition and the next steps. We are saddened by the outcome of this situation, but we have full confidence that we will come together as a community of allies for successful transition. ADSD is planning to hire temporary staff to continue the services of language acquisition, telecommunications equipment and assistive technology distribution, and access to services until new community partners are selected through the previously scheduled competitive Notice of Funding Opportunity . We would like to thank ADSD for supporting the Board’s final decision. Please know that the Board does not take this decision lightly. For more questions or updates on current transition plans, please contact: Adrienne Navarro, Advocacy and Community Services Chief amnavarro@adsd.nv.gov Sal Fiorentino, Communication Access Services Program Manager VP: 830-9103 Email: SFiorentino@adsd.nv.gov Again, if you need us, we are here for you until December 30, 2020, so feel free to contact us. Thank you, take care. Bye bye.

Lynette Boyer, a DeafBlind woman, and Sally Prouty, a hearing woman, didn’t expect to become leaders in the fight for adequate communication and information support services for people who are deafblind. Thanks to their advocacy, Minnesota became one of the few states to pay for interveners for children and Support Service Providers for adults who were deafblind. Ten years later, they again advocated for funding to expand services. Interveners help children become independent, and SSPs work with adults who are deafblind by providing auditory, visual and environmental information. In 1997, Prouty, the mother of a deafblind son, and Boyer, a deafblind woman and the Minnesota DeafBlind Association president, convinced legislators to support a bill that funded interveners and SSPs. Together, they brought in people to testify about their successes in working with interveners and SSPs. They also mobilized parents and other parties and helped educate legislators by sharing their experiences. The bill passed. What helped Boyer’s and Prouty’s efforts were how they shared their experiences. Boyer talked about how SSPs and interveners allowed her to be independent. Prouty talked about how her family moved to Minnesota from Utah, and described Utah’s statewide system for interveners. Prouty also talked about how her son became independent in Utah thanks to this program, and how no such program existed in Minnesota. As a result of the bill’s passage, Minnesota established a pilot program that had a large number of requests for service. Today, the program continues to receive funding, and programs continue to expand.

Hello everyone! Welcome to my channel. If you’re new, my name is Rachel, this is my name sign . This is my ASL vlog. Hi everyone! Welcome back! I know I haven't vlogged in a few days, but I'm posting all of them at the same time anyway so today is Thursday, I forgot what day it is, it's Thursday. I just finished finals. I have one more on Monday, but that's for a P.E. class so it's easy. I don't need to study for it. So I wanted to let you know what's been happening. I thought I had a final for ASL 4 this morning, so I sat down with my laptop and got ready. I went on the website. All the tests for that class are online. I pulled it up and saw there was no test posted. I couldn't find it anywhere. So I texted my friend and asked her if she could find the test. She answered "What?! There's no test. We're done. Last week's test was the final." I was like what?! I misunderstood. There was no final, the test last week was the final. So I'm finished with that class. After that I went to the Fingerspelling final. The teacher told us that our receptive test wasn't ready yet. It's online, but we were going to take it together in the ASL lab. But since it wasn't ready, so she's going to post it online tonight and we have the weekend to take it. I'm a little bit annoyed because I just wanted to be done with everything and not have to worry about anything. But our expressive skills test was ready so we took that, so I'm done with that, but I still have to take the receptive test. After that I went to my comics class which was the big project. It wasn't a test. I had to draw and design a comic myself. I worked on it this whole week all day everyday. That's why I haven't been vlogging. That's finally done! I finished last night around 10:00 which sounds late, but it's earlier than before. Some days I was going to sleep around 3am. So I'm finally done. The comic looks really nice so I'm happy. It was a lot of work. When I got to class, everyone had their comics laid out on the table. Then we all took one of each comic. It was exciting to read other people's comics. I kept a few of my comics to give to my family so they can read it. So now I'm in the music room at the piano. I can finally relax and it feels so nice. I've been working so continuously the last few weeks. So I'm excited to finally relax and work on other projects like YouTube and my book. I want to post a video about my book soon to explain about it. If you don't know, I'm writing a book. It's fiction. So yeah. I will post a video soon about that to let you know what I'm doing. And just other different things I'm working on. So yeah. Now I realized after Fingerspelling class ended, I won't have to sign for anything else. All my ASL classes are finished. There are no more Deaf events until January. I think there's one in January in my home town. I'm excited to go to that and see a lot of people I haven't seen in a long time, but it's almost a month before that. I really need to practice. School starts January 21st. I come back January 20th and the interpreting application is due just around 2 weeks later. So I really need to practice during the break. I'm going to really try to vlog every day and watch videos. I'm going to see if I can meet some of my friends from back home from my ASL classes before. I don't know, a lot of people are busy at Christmas time. So I understand if we can't. But I have to practice. I have one friend here that I practice with. She lives far away but we discussed maybe using FaceTime to practice or something. I don't know. We're both going to try to keep practicing through winter break. So that's what's been happening recently. Thank you for watching. Subscribe to see more. Let me know in the comments if you have feedback on my signing. Also like this video and I will see you later. Bye!

Hi, my name is Alexis Nelson I'm going to tell you my experience with the Deaf Community Service event I never truly understood the Deaf Community until I was able to mix around with and have casual conversation with Deaf people. How did the community service impact me? I learned that not all Deaf children know ASL. Most of the Deaf children at the event had a cochlear implant. They spoke English. All of us played a bunch of different games with the kids. The kids learned a few basic signs. This made me feel happy that I could help them even a little big of sign. I really enjoyed teaching the children the colors and watching them soak in all the information. They were all interested. They all wanted to learn. I realized that many Deaf people who have cochlear implants, never learn ASL. I feel that their Deaf Identity is suppressed with cochlear implants. It makes me sad that no one taught these kids ASL, the Deaf Community, their true identity They don't know I loved this experience I will never forget it. Bye!

It’s Tuesday now so it’s time for another plant tip! What are your leaves trying to tell you? We often get inquiries about leaves. A general rule of thumb is most issues can be categorized into two categories: yellowing and browning leaves. When leaves turn yellow it is usually a sign of over watering. However, there can be three causes behind browning leaves. Firstly, watering directly on the leaves causing brown spots to form. Secondly, chemicals in the water causing leaf burn. Thirdly, it’s a sign of under watering as plants wither of thirst. There’s no saving a leaf that’s turned yellow or brown. It is recommended you cut off those leaves so the plants can redirect all its energy into healthy leaves. Best of luck!

ALEXANDRIA WAILES: “Decide Who You Are” was created in 1992 and is a series of various works by the Conceptual artist Adrian Piper, a first generation and foundational member of the movement. The artist focused on abuses of power in institutions, especially as they unfold along lines of race, gender, and class. The works function as large triptychs with three sections; Each work is just over 6 feet high by nearly 12 feet long, each featuring a historical photograph or group of images at the center. This work, “Decide Who You Are #19: Torch Song Alert,” focuses on race and class discrimination and conflict, referencing events that were recent to the making of the artwork. At center, three framed black-and-white magazine and newspaper portraits depict politicians implicated in banking scandals in the early 1990s. The documentary photograph below depicts a scene from the 1991 riots in Crown Heights, Brooklyn. The left panel of each work in the series includes a drawing of the three wise monkeys, a Japanese pictorial maxim embodying the proverbial principle: “See no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil.” Hovering above the monkeys are Piper’s own typewritten meditations on various kinds of oppressions. Specifically addressing corporate greed, this text begins: “TORCH SONG ALERT. Immovable in the face of rational appeal, Impervious to the voice of conscience, Dancing around to the tune of deregulated accumulation, You force our hand to press the button.” The right panel of each work is filled from top to bottom with a running transcription of macro- and microaggressions experienced by Piper— snippets from conversations and comments made by her colleagues. Some read: “It’s fine.” “I don’t know what you mean.” “It wasn’t intentional.” “Stop getting emotional.” “You’ll learn to see things differently.” Around 150 short phrases cover the entire panel. At the bottom, screen-printed beneath the red text, is a black-and-white portrait of a young Anita Hill, who would later become the Black lawyer to testify in 1991 against Supreme Court nominee Clarence Thomas, alleging sexual harassment. Some of the text printed across Hill’s face includes: “That’s crazy. I don’t believe that happened. Why bring this up?” Here and throughout this series, Piper draws from public and private encounters, using photographic records and constructed text to recount histories of oppression that were heretofore undocumented, or if already documented, left untold or cast aside.

text_english: My name is Myranda Bradley. My Story is named "Soaked With Water." Two summers ago, I was in my hometown. Every year my church youth group has a fundraiser. They put on a car wash. The kids take buckets, fill them with water, and add soap. S-P-O-N-G-E. They put sponges into the buckets and use them to wash the cars. The cars line up. The kids in youth group are split up into many teams. One works together to wash a car. The next car is washed by another team. I helped, but I was staff. S-T-A-F-F-because I am not in high school, but in college. Many friends my age came to help. They are also staff. When the fundraiser started, I went to the church. Some friends washed cars and some used the hoses to make the cars wet so others could begin washing. One of my friends was using the hose to spray the cars. Sometimes when kids mess with him, he would spray them as they were washing cars. I started to think about something I could do. "Maybe I'll get the hose and spray him. Then see what happens." I"m only mischievous sometimes. I looked around and saw a kid who had a hose. I walked over to him and asked, " Can I borrow the hose?" He handed it to me. I waited and waited. When my friend was not looking in my direction, I slowly and carefully walked up and sprayed him from behind. When the water hit him, he tensed up. His face looked like he was a bit surprised. He turned around and looked at me. I saw him look at me and though, "I need to run and escape" But I didn't make it. He ran up caught me. He grabbed the hose. When he grabbed the hose, he lifted it above my head. The water came down on me. I was soaked. It flowed down and I covered my face. My whole body was wet. My friend who had the hose walked away. I was really really wet. I'm not sorry. Because when I sprayed him, he made a funny face. I laughed really hard. That day, I learned when I tease a friend, if they are a boy, They will tease me 3-5 times worse. I need to be careful. Next time I need an escape plan. I need a better one.

text_english: Hello everyone I'm Sign1News anchor Jethro Wooddall here's a quick look at the top trending stories making headlines today if you post an interesting comment during this update we may add it at the bottom of the screen now let's see what's happening in the news today we have an update to the situation unfolding at Atlanta area school for the deaf student leaders are protesting the hiring of a new superintendent who is hearing and they also want to divest from the control of the Georgia Department of Education a confidential source told Sign1News a meeting was scheduled to take place today Wednesday between the DOE and student leaders but the school is closed for the next two weeks due to Covid so students were asked to send a PowerPoint presentation instead the students refused out of concerns that meeting will be canceled as well another source tells Sign1News the students were threatened with zero grades if they continued the protest Sign1News has also learned there has been a major shakeup in staffing at the school when former superintendent John Serrano who is deaf left AASD dozens of teachers and staff members left as well Sign1News producers reached out to the department of education to hear their side we received a response today from Meghan Frick director of communications who says the director of the state schools divisions Doctor Kenny Moore will be meeting with students to discuss their concerns and released a statement which reads in part we cannot violate individual's privacy by speaking in detail about personnel or hiring decisions however we can state that the hiring committee worked to select the best candidate based on their past employment history experience and other qualifications additionally safeguarding students from Covid 19 exposure has nothing to do with the students decision to protest the Georgia Department of education stands opposed to audism and other forms of prejudice and the Atlanta area school for the deaf has had both hearing and deaf hard of hearing superintendents in the past students participating in a peaceful protest has provided school officials an opportunity to teach students that peaceful protests is an important part of our nation's civic history at 12:01 AM Friday the federal government is set to run out of funding that could mean the country would not be able to pay its bills come mid October resulting in a government shutdown so far Democrats and Republicans have been split on the best way to proceed but a source who attended a Democratic caucus meeting says a vote could happen as soon as today that would fund the government through December 3 this is video of 32-year-old Stephen Sloan Junior being handcuffed and escorted off a flight to Hawaii after punching a flight attendant multiple times Hawaiian air officials say the assault was not triggered by noncompliance with mask wearing policies but the assault did happen on the same day aviation industry leaders went before Congress to testify about an alarming surge nationally in unruly airline passengers with nearly 4300 incidents and 750 investigations this year alone for the first time since 1954 a human case of rabies in the state of Illinois resulted in a death wildlife officials say a bat colony was living in 87-year-old Thomas Krobs' attic last month in the middle of the night Krob woke up with a bat biting on his neck officials from the Department of Health say the bat tested positive for rabies but Krob did not want treatment a month later he started having neck pain headaches numbness in his fingers difficulty controlling his arms trouble speaking and died this week America's top military commanders faced a contentious grilling from lawmakers on Capitol Hill they testified publicly for the first time about the deadly and chaotic withdrawal from Afghanistan earlier this month Secretary of Defense Lloyd Austin defended the exit testifying the US did not believe Afghanistan would fall the way that it did in a matter of days former President Obama finally broke ground on his presidential library in Chicago after months of delays by lawsuits and local complaints including a legal action by environmentalists who argued public land should not be used for private projects a federal judge dismissed that lawsuit in his speech Obama said he hopes the facility will help strengthen democratic ideals help bridge political divisions in the country and he went down memory lane recalling how his political career got a kickstart in the Windy City a dog named Maggie dying from cancer enjoyed snow for the first time in Utah Monday Maggie's owner contacted the staff at the Salt Lake County Ice Center they gathered ice shavings bins and buckets and placed the ice in Maggie's backyard for her and her family to enjoy unfortunately Maggie has since passed away but officials say she got to enjoy some of her final moments with her family and some snow Sign1News will be broadcasting live updates Monday through Friday then this Saturday at 6 PM Eastern time join us for a live panel of diverse guests to discuss the hot topics of the week again be sure to post your thoughts and text_english: What's the sign?

Hello everyone. My name is Dr. Darlene Zangara. I am thrilled to have been chosen to be the new Executive Director at the Minnesota Commission of the Deaf, DeafBlind & Hard of Hearing . I’d like to thank Governor Tim Walz, Lieutenant Governor Peggy Flanagan, the community members who participated in the online search process survey, the Search Committee members, the Interview Committee members, the MNCDHH board, and the staff for all of their hard work during the Executive Director Search Process. I’d also like to thank my colleagues at the Olmstead Implementation Office and Minnesota Housing & Finance Agency for their support. For the past five and a half years, I served as the Executive Director for the Olmstead Implementation Office . OIO’s mission is to ensure that people with disabilities live their best lives. Through OIO, I worked with a subcabinet from 13 state agencies appointed by the Governor, first Governor Dayton and then Governor Walz. By working together, we carried out a statewide plan, partnering with people and organizations from all over Minnesota to ensure that all voices are heard and included. I also provided education and resources to our legislators and developed relationships at the Capitol. Thanks to 1) my experiences at OIO, 2) my previous work and volunteer experiences with nonprofit organizations and community organizations, and 3) my relationships with state agencies, community organizations, and legislators around the state I feel ready and excited to work at MNCDHH! During my first few months at MNCDHH, I plan to spend time with board members, Commission staff, deaf, deafblind, and hard of hearing community members, parents of children who are deaf, deafblind, and hard of hearing, legislators and policy makers, and partner agencies to enhance our relationships and develop stronger partnerships. All of our stakeholders have an important part in MNCDHH’s mission, which is to remove systemic barriers in Minnesota. Together, we can ensure that deaf, deafblind, and hard of hearing Minnesotans enjoy communication access and equal opportunity. Please follow MNCDHH’s newsletter, website, and social media for additional updates on our work and progress. Thank you!

To help prevent the spread of Covid-19 and initiate a safe and healthy start to the spring semester, RIT is strongly encouraging all students, faculty, and staff, regardless of immunization status, to get a COVID-19 test 2-3 days before returning to campus in January. As we approach another holiday season in the pandemic, take precautions to enjoy travel and holiday fun. COVID-19 transmission usually happens at indoor social gatherings where people are not wearing masks. Here are a few tips to share health and well-being as well as holiday cheer: Wear a mask when socializing indoors, especially when you are in the company of those who may not be fully vaccinated or could be vulnerable to serious illness. Bundle up and go for a walk or play outside with friends and loved ones instead of staying indoors. Spend quality one-on-one time with friends rather than in large gatherings. Make some video calls. Get a booster if six months beyond your final Pfizer or Moderna dose or two months out from your J&J dose. COVID-19 will be here for a while, and we are learning to live with it. Remember, these safety measures protect vulnerable members of our communities, families, and ourselves. As global citizens, it is vitally important for us to look out for one another. Get a COVID-19 test 2-3 days before your return to ensure results come in before you leave home. Isolating at home is more comfortable than navigating roommates for many of us. If you are a student and test positive, please upload your result to the Wellness Portal in the Health Center to update your professors regarding a remote start. If you are a faculty or staff and test positive, consult with your physician and report this information in RIT Employee Self–Service. We will continue to update the community via the RIT Safety Plan. Thank you for your continued diligence and commitment to preserving the health of our community. Happy & healthy holidays!

text_english: Hello Everyone! How are you? I'm doing good! I’m Marcelle, your Membership Director of Bay Area Black Deaf Advocates . I’m very excited to announce that we will have an awesome, fun-filled event happening on Friday, June 19, 2020! BABDA will host a Juneteenth event on FB Live. What is Juneteenth about? Juneteenth is a Black Freedom Day where we we celebrate our official freedom from slavery. This celebration is a cultural alternative to the 4th of July holiday. Our Juneteenth event will have plenty of activities, starting with a “bring-your-own-drink” happy hour, facts trivia game about Juneteenth, ASL Storytelling, entertainment and family activities. All people, regardless of race, are welcome to virtually join us and celebrate Juneteenth with our Black community. Again, don’t forget to mark your calendar for Friday, June 19 at 6 PM - 8 PM . Please feel free to contact us at babdaofficers@gmail.com if you have questions. See you all there on FB Live ! Thank you for watching. Enjoy your day. See ya!

text_english: MDSC members had many opportunities to present its case in person during its three-year battle with the Public Utilities Commission. MDSC leaders recognized that writing letters by itself would not be good enough. They decided a strong physical presence would make a lasting impression on members of the Public Utilities Commission. More than 40 MDSC members attended every administrative hearing held by the Public Utilities Commission over three years. In all, MDSC members and ASL interpreters attended dozens of meetings. There was no way that the Commission could ignore the MDSC presence! For many on the Commission, it was their first introduction to ASL and its importance to the deaf community. Their presence made a strong impact. During the three-year process, MDSC members presented testimony in ASL, submitted legal briefs, gathered and presented petitions, and shared new facts and statistics to support their case. It was an amazing effort, especially when you remember that most members had never advocated for anything before and certainly had never dreamt of being part of something this big!

text_english: Hello Deaf Community! We are so happy to share this news with you! We've missed you since March 2020! Imagine, one year and four months, right?! Approximately that long, but want to share with you that starting today, July 1st , staff will be returning to their offices. But, I want to explain the plan so you know what is going on and what to expect. While staff are returning to the offices today, many will follow what we call a hybrid schedule, which means three days at the office and two days remotely. The reason for that is because since working remotely due to COVID-19, it shows we can work remotely. some staff want to continue with that option and we need to provide that, same as with different businesses all over the world. The business world has changed because of COVID-19, and same for us at DHHSC. Some staff will continue working remotely on some days. Some staff who are vaccinated for COVID-19 can work everyday at the office if they want, so you might see them at the office everyday, but some staff might want to still work remotely on some days. Will see a variation in schedules, but remember three days at the office, two days remotely. That's the plan. We have to get used to being back in the office so we can know what to expect and how to keep things safe for our community. Starting in August, on August 2nd Monday actually, only client with an appointment can come to the office. Not just come into the lobby and socialize because it is not safe, but if you have an appointment ready, you can come into the office. If you do not yet have an appointment but come into the office, you can make an appointment but will have to leave and come back later at your appointment time. We want to restrict the number of people in the building, for your safety too. When you arrive at the office, make sure you have your appointment already scheduled. You must also have either a vaccination card or a mask. But want to share that if you already vaccinated but still prefer to wear a mask, that is fine. It's up to you but we want you to feel safe. But, if you are not vaccinated, you must wear a mask. If you are vaccinated and do not want to wear a mask, you must bring your vaccination card with you to show proof that you have been vaccinated. We will be asking each person who comes into the office to show proof of vaccination. If you do not want to show your vaccination card, you must wear a mask. We have to ask for proof, same as with our own staff. We will be following common procedures. I have already checked with Cal/OSHA and Sierra HR Partners to ask questions about how to keep our staff and community safe. I want you to be prepared to bring your vaccination card if you do not want to wear a mask. Starting in August, too for the Fresno HQ, we will allow 10-15 people in our community room for classes, workshops, and art activities. We will start small, 10-15 people, because the community room is spacious and will allow us to seat people comfortably apart to keep everyone as safe as possible. Later, as we continue to monitor the COVID-19 situation, because we know people are worried about the Delta Variant, we are not comfortable hosting big events yet. For example, we will not have Valley Deaf Festival this year and postponed it to next year in 2022. We want to host VDF in person and are excited about hosting it in person in 2022. For the Holiday Luncheon, it will probably be a drive through event where you can pick up your food and go. We want to find ways to celebrate being with our community because we miss you! We look forward to seeing you again, starting in August. But, for now, please continue contacting us for services because we are still serving via videopone , Zoom, home visits, or site visits . Whatever you need, please continue contacting us for services. We are here for you! Thank you for your support!

Hello! My name is Neil McDevitt and I'm the Executive Director here at DHCC I'm here at the DHCC office because we're starting to get ready to gradually and slowly reopen In the early days of COVID-19 here in Philadelphia, we did several videos to highlight what Deaf, hard of hearing, and DeafBlind folks needed to know. Now, it’s June and we’re in the yellow phase. There are different colors: Red, Yellow and Green. But some folks may be confused as to what the colors actually mean. From March until last week, June 5, 2020, most of the Philadelphia region was in the Red. Philadelphia and its surrounding counties moved into the Yellow Phase last week. The colors are really related to businesses. For example, under Red, Yellow or Green, a food store is an essential business and that will remain open. Same as a hospital or doctors office. They’re essential organizations. A hairdresser or a gym would be closed under Red or Yellow but will be allowed to reopen under green but only allow half of the customers they would normally have. Restaurants are take-out or delivery only during the red phase. They may move to outdoor dining only in yellow phase, and then in green phase they can have indoor dining with half capacity. It's all a bit confusing! What do the phases mean for you as an individual? We still need to wear our masks or face coverings. We still need to wash our hands. We still need to sanitize our hands. We still need to clean the surfaces we touch. Even if we’re in a “Green” area, COVID-19 is still a risk for us. Which means that until the Center for Disease Control change their rules, we will still probably need to wear those masks! The virus will no longer be a danger to us when a vaccine is widely available and we have a lot of people vaccinated. That will be when we can stop wearing masks. But it’s important to clean our hands and surfaces regularly! I know, it’s not what we want to see. But it’s important for us to keep each other safe! Thank You!

text_english: Hi everyone! Welcome to my channel. I know it's been a while since I uploaded a video, but there are a few things I want to discuss today. The first thing is a recap on fall semester. I'll talk about what it was like, what I learned, etc. If you're new, I'm in the CSUN interpreting program. Last semester was my first semester in the program. I just want to discuss what we did and what I learned and things like that. The second thing I want to talk about, is what next semester will look like and what I anticipate doing. The third thing I want to discuss is what this channel is going to look like from now on. So things like when I upload. Unfortunately that is going to change. The fourth thing is, I have an exciting announcement! So that's what I want to discuss today, let's get started! So last semester was my first semester in the interpreting program and the semester is now over. It was hard. It was really hard. The work took a lot of time and we had to practice a lot. It was really important to put a lot of time into it We focused on consecutive interpreting this semester. That is where someone signs and then stops and you interpret it. When you are done interpreting, they will continue. It can also be a person speaking. We learned using videos, so we would watch a video, then it would pause automatically and we would film ourselves interpreting. When we were done interpreting, the video would start again, and we would continue watching the next section. We also learned about mental processing and how to translate between languages in our minds. It was very basic interpreting things. It was difficult, but I know it will only become harder as the program goes on. My class practices and studies together a lot which is really nice. That helped me a lot. Now let's talk about next semester. We're going to start learning simultaneous interpreting. That means there will be someone signing and we will be voice interpreting at the same time as they're signing. Or someone will be speaking and we will interpret into ASL at the same time. That is going to be very hard. The teacher told us that it's going to be very difficult. Their goal is to see how we will handle the reality of interpreting and how difficult it is for the future when we're working in real situations. If we're going to get stressed and overwhelmed, and not going to be able to continue interpreting, that's something we need to learn now. They need to see that we can handle interpreting and not break down. So for that reason, it's going to be very intense. But I'm ready. I've been practicing through winter break. But I should practice more. My class has been practicing together, but they're practicing simultaneous interpreting now but I still need to work on consecutive so I've been mostly practicing on my own. Later on, I hope to start practicing simultaneous with them. So the next thing to discuss is this channel. As I said, next semester will be very difficult. I have a lot of personal projects and things that I enjoy working on and the reality is, I cannot continue to work on all of them. I need to focus a lot on school, so I will have to give up some things and reduce the amount of time I spend on other things. Unfortunately, that means I will not be spending as much time on this channel. I know I already haven't been super consistent and unfortunately, I will probably be posting even less. I apologize for that. I wish I could manage both this and school but I know that's unrealistic. I'm not going to stop posting completely, I will try to still upload some, but I don't know how often. I might be able to upload once a month, but I don't feel like I can say that right now because I don't know what's going to happen. I don't know what next semester is going to be like, but I just wanted to let you know that I will be posting less. I will try to post when I can Finally, my announcement. I'm moving! I'm going back to CSUN. Next semester will still continue online because of COVID. However, I decided to move back anyway for a few reasons. The first reason is, I need to focus on school. Right now, I'm living with my family and they take up a lot of my time. My focus is really divided between family, friends, work, and there's just a lot going on. If I go back to CSUN, I only have, like, two friends there, we're close, but still. I don't have a job there right now, I might get one in the future, we'll see. But it's just overall a better environment for me to focus Another reason is most of my interpreting program lives there. Definitely not all of them, but most do. And they've been able to meet in person a few times. They're safe, they wear masks and observe social distancing, but they still get to see each other and I want to be involved in that. Another reason I want to go back is because college only happens once. Well not for some people, but for me. Once I graduate, that's it. I don't plan on going back to college and I want to experience it. I feel like I'm missing out. I feel like I'm stuck here and I'm missing everything. There aren't a lot of people my age in this area. I want to feel like I'm involved in college life. And I know that we can't socialize and go around people, but living close to the college and being in that area, I feel like I'm closer and as involved as I can be. So those are my reasons for moving back. So that means I will be vlogging all of that. So I have all those videos planned and I'll try to upload them before school starts. I leave here January 17th and then school starts a week later. The first video I will make is a packing vlog. After that I'll do a moving in video and an apartment tour. I'm actually going to be living alone in an apartment. I can't live with my roommate from last year because her parents prefer for her to stay in the dorms but because of COVID, the school is only letting a few people stay in the dorms, so I can't. I found a small studio apartment off campus So I'll be living there. I'll do a tour of the apartment and then I'm contemplating doing a fourth video. I have notebook where I've been writing meal plans and grocery lists and where I plan to budget, adult things like that. Just me learning how to do that stuff because before I lived in the dorms and we had the dining hall, so I didn't go grocery shopping. But now, I'm completely on my own, so I have to figure out all the adult things. So I might make a video about that. We'll see. So that's pretty much what's going to be happening on this channel from now on. Thank you for watching. Please like and subscribe to my channel if you enjoyed this and comment below if you have questions or ideas for other videos you want to see. I will still continue to upload, but it will be less often. Thank you for watching and I will see you later. Bye!

text_english: DON'T-DO-THAT

Vision board. What is that? Why should you make one? How do you start? I will cover all of that here. A vision board is simply a visual reminder of what you want out of life. Your true desires. Your goals. All of them are in a display on a visual map. The map can be tactile, like on paper with pictures or drawings on it. Or it can be digital on your phone or as a computer background. Something that you can frequently look at as a constant reminder. The vision board can be set up for the next three months. It can be project-based. It can be a yearly theme such as "My 2020 vision board". It can be for as long as five years or your entire lifetime. It can be your ultimate vision board for your life. Why do people make vision boards? The fact is because it works. Often, successful people like Olympic athletes do use that tool. They visualize themselves doing that sport, and in their mind they think, "I see myself doing it." Or presenters will visualize seeing themselves standing on a stage and talking to an audience. Or a hiker will visualize seeing themselves standing on the top of a mountain. It works. Do you know why? Our brains are powerful. Take the opportunity to use the brain as a tool to apply that towards our lives. There are many amazing stories of people making vision boards and then successfully achieving those visions. There are many. I even have my own stories. For example, before my first career I had written down on paper that I wanted to work in that type of career and left it alone. Then a few years later, I found myself in that exact same job that I had visualized about. One of my friends printed a picture of the Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco. She put it up on her vision board. Then over time, she packed up and moved, got a job, and then she moved into her new apartment. As she was unpacking, she found her vision board and she realized that the bridge in the picture was now her bedroom window view. She could look outside and see the Golden Gate Bridge. Amazing. There are so many stories like that. I usually tell people that their lives are like a blank canvas that is waiting for them to paint it however they want, with colors, with pictures. It's truly up to you. The fact is all of us have 24 hours in a day. What will you do with those 24 hours? How will you use them? What will you spend them on? On who? Those 24 hours a day may seem like nothing, but as they pass by each day, those hours accumulate and end up being your whole life. Your identity. So, do you really know what you want? Do you know what it looks like? The shape you are molding your life into? Do you really know? Often, we are all distracted, busy, going through the motions and living day to day. We are often near-sighted with our lives and only living in the 'now'. We need to stretch our vision further to be able to visualize what we want our lives to look like in the future. Often, people tend to struggle with that. I get it. I am the same. Having a vision board is the activity, the exercise, the experiment for you to really look beyond and determine how you can shape your actions, your decisions, and time so that you can form a path to get there. You can live with more intention. That's really the bottom line of a vision board. How do you start? There are good and bad news for this. There are no rules. There's no right or wrong way to do it. This may seem like bad news for some of you as you are probably waiting for direct instructions, to give you structure and guidance. But it's actually good news because you can't mess up your vision board. You really can't. It's impossible. Vision boards are one of those activities where you learn through the process itself. The process teaches you to look within and to learn how to adapt. I do have my own tips that I'll offer you based on my few years of experience and experimenting with my own boards. First, I think it's important to start with the right mindset. For example, do you think it's a good idea to be lounging around on the couch playing on your phone and then getting up and going straight to running a 5K? No. You need to warm up first, right? This is in proportion to making a vision board. You need to warm up your mind, your emotions, and your soul for this type of mental and spiritual exercise. So how to do it? Personally, I like to start with a bit of yoga, stretching, listening to your body and recognizing where you feel tense. Then you stretch out those tense areas. At the same time, quieten your mind. Make sure your phone is put away. Make sure you have a good chunk of time - I like putting aside three to four hours. Make sure there are no distractions, and that commitments and responsibilities are settled so to prevent any interruptions. Then you can really focus your attention on yourself and on your internal process. You could meditate for around ten minutes. During that time, try to push aside any distractions. Ask yourself questions. For example, what kind of perfect day would I design if I could? What would that day look like? Where would you go on your dream vacation? Who do I want to meet? What do I want to check off in my bucket list? What habits do I need to let go of? What are the changes and improvements that I need to make? There are some of the various kinds of questions you can ask yourself and see what answers come up. After that is done, then the equally important next step would be to give yourself permission. You could think, write, or even sign. You could look at the mirror or say it to someone else or tell it to yourself. "I give myself permission to dream, to listen to what I truly want." "I give myself permission to play with my imagination, without judging myself, or self-critiquing, or thinking it's ridiculous, or that I can't do it, or that it's unrealistic, or putting myself down." All of that has no place in this activity. This activity is about you paying attention to what feels good, what feels right, and the warm feeling inside. Pay attention to the light shining inside you. Pursue it without fear, judgement, and self-criticism. Then you are ready, you are warmed up and about to start. It's also important to have some materials. For example, having a stack of magazines that fit your topics of interest as those likely will have pictures of what you are looking for. Maybe you can print pictures from online. You will also need some kind of firm paper. I like using scrapbook pages as a base as they have different patterns and colors. You can have stickers, markers, pens, glue, and tape. It's up to you if you want to add a mixture of media like fabric or your own photos. Again, there is no right or wrong to this. There are no rules. It's about what works for you. After you have everything ready, then just go for it. Listen to your intuition. Skim through magazines, pictures and see what pops out to you and catches your eye. If you feel these are right for you, then go ahead and cut them out until you feel satisfied. Then you can start placing them, gluing, and writing notes of what your goals are. There's a range. Some people are more abstract and feeling-based. They use quotes and focus on mood. Some people are on the other end of the spectrum. They like to be more specific and measurable. For example, they can set a goal to run once a week, or set a goal to save $5,000. It's really a broad spectrum. I personally think it's healthy to have a mix within that range. Now that you've put everything on your board and it's ready to be put up. The last step is where to put it. It should be put in a place where you can see it every day. It can be in your bedroom, bathroom, or office. Why you need to display your board is because it is a visual reminder of what kind of life you want to have. Who you want to become. You will often see it and have constant reminders which become a part of your consciousness. You will naturally shape your actions, your choices, your thoughts, and your time. It will be a natural transition to capture what you see on the board. It is important that what you see on your board makes you feel good. As that is what you want to pursue. For me, in the past I used to be overwhelmed on what to do with my time. As I was trying to figure out if I should use that time I had to watch a movie, or go shopping, or go for a walk, or contact my friends to see them, or... What was I supposed to do with my time? The vision board helped to prompt me. It was my anchor to my truth. That is what a vision board is for me.

As of February 10, 2020, the Minnesota Deaf Substance Use Disorder Treatment Program is integrated with the Lodging Plus Program at M Health Fairview. This program is 28 days long and serves DHH people who need residential treatment. ASL interpreting services will be provided for 30 hours a week. During the weekday, evenings, and on weekends, there will be at least one Deaf Drug Unit Assistant providing outside sober activities and taking DHH clients to 12-step meetings. A Deaf counselor will work up to 8 hours a week assisting DHH clients as needed. This program offers individual and group therapy, lectures, workshops, skills groups, recreational activities, spiritual care groups, yoga, acupuncture, interpreted 12-step meetings, comprehensive assessment services and aftercare planning. We would like to see more referrals to increase enrollment and demonstrate the need for this program. If we have a larger number of DHH clients in treatment, that will increase our grant opportunities with the ultimate goal to have the specialized DDBHH SUD Treatment Program open again. Treatment for DHH at M Health Fairview in Lodging Plus accepts private insurance, and Medicaid and consolidated funding so if you have any of those, your treatment is paid for you. If you have Medicare, 80% of the treatment is covered by Medicare and 20% through Medicaid or private insurance. Thirdly, farmer's market where many people bring their own products to sell such as vegetables or fruits and such. Another example can be at libraries or religious services such as church or temple. text_english: Hello, welcome back. My name is Leeza. This video again is funded by First 5 of Merced County. This is apart of the Parenting Video Series. Today's topic we will discuss "Babysitters". When you start thinking about getting a babysitter for your child, it is a positive experience because it creates a support system. You are able to rely on someone to watch your kids while you are working and/or running errands. You should plan with trust. It takes time and requires planning to prepare for the future. When thinking about getting a babysitter, you need to think if the need will be just for a couple of hours or if it will be more than a few hours. When you start getting ready to pick a babysitter, you will want to let your child/children know what is happening. Explain to them, "Mom is leaving to work for a few hours". Make sure to communicate that. I want to emphasize that. Also if your child is over the age of 2, have your child involved in helping pick who their babysitter will be. When searching for a babysitter, you can ask your friends or family for their advice and feedback on someone they can recommend. If that doesn't work, you can look online at Care.com . You can put down exactly what you are looking for. If you need someone who is CPR certified or want someone with a background check. Also if you want you or your child to be matched ahead of time with a babysitter based off of your child's needs. Suppose you have a Deaf child and you want the babysitter to be able to sign as well. You will also want to check their background to make sure they are qualified. Also it is important to check their references. This means when the potential babysitter gives you their references, you will need to call those numbers to get more information about that person's qualifications and history. That way you know more about them. Questions to ask the babysitter would be - Have you taken care of children the same age as mine? Do you have any disabilities? Do you know ASL? I'm curious why do you want to be a babysitter? How much do you charge? Do you have any additional trainings or certifications? Did you go to college and take Child Development courses? Also it is important to ask, What are your plans with my child/children while I am gone? Ask some good questions. When you make your final decision picking your babysitter, remember to have your child/children meet them. Invite them over to eat together and explain that this will be a person who will come over when you are at work or out running errands. It is important to explain to your child that this person can be trusted. Before leaving, a good topic discussion with the babysitter would be about food, sleeping schedule, discipline and different activities to do. Make sure to plan for the day. This information you can look on First 5 of Merced Counties website. If you have any questions you can email us and let us know. Also if you want to schedule an appointment with us. I wanted to say thank you for watching. Bye, see you next time!
