text_english: Hello, hello. I’m really thrilled. I’m Joel with DeafNation Live. I’m thrilled to have two guests with me. Where are they from? Canada! All the way up in Quebec *the sign for Quebec* We’re gonna discuss about LSQ. We’ll elaborate on that shortly, but before we do, I’d like to introduce you two! What are your names? I’m Cynthia Benoit *C-Y-C-Y* I’m from Ottawa My name is Daz Saunders *D-Z * I’m from Montreal Quebec. It is the biggest city in Quebec. Yes, yes. I just want to make sure I’m clear with what languages I’ll be using during the interview. I’ll use ASL and a little bit of International Sign and LSQ. But mostly ASL during this discussion. I’d like all of us to keep an open mind. So today we’ll focus on LSQ. Is there other sign languages in Canada? Yes, they have ASL and LSQ. That’s exactly why I invited you both on the show. To discuss what’s happening in Canada and specifically in Quebec. Could you expand on what LSQ is? Go ahead Cynthia, you may start. LSQ stands for Quebec Sign Language. It emerged and is distinct to Quebec. While ASL is mainly used in most parts of North America, LSQ is prevalent in Quebec. Interestingly enough, ASL has its own history. It originates from the roots of LSF originating from France intertwined with MVSL . LSQ has his own history as well! While ASL expanded, not the modern ASL as we know it, but the “old ASL” the original version that is. Don’t forget that sign languages were used in Deaf Schools. And that LSF came from France. ASL was primarily used at the Deaf School for girls, while LSF were mainly used at the school for boys. A little of BSL influenced the making of it. As time went by, there was social interaction between students of both schools. Eventually a third language emerged. To this day, it is known as LSQ. This is LSQ’s history and why there is some similarities between ASL and LSQ. It is due to its origins even though there’s still evidential differences between them Similar roots It’s interesting to see how ASL has influences from LSF and other sign languages. Like for example, h ow would you sign “normal”? * shows sign for “normal” * Normal, normal, normal In France, they sign “normal” this way. Normal is an International Sign. Kinda similar-ish. Kind of, yes. That’s very interesting. That’s exactly why I’m glad I’ve got you both to appear on this show to demystify LSQ and because of the decline of LSQ as a language. Personally, I love different sign languages from all over the world. Earlier, we mentioned MVSL, which stands for Martha’s Vineyard Sign Language. What’s all that about? Well Martha’s Vineyard is actually an island of historical significance in the shaping and developing of ASL. Don’t go anywhere. We will go into more details right back after these messages. Welcome back! I’m eager to lean more. I’ve always found it stimulating to learn about the different sign languages. Alright, let’s get on with it. There’s a picture I’d like to show you. That’s quite a crowd. Looks like there’s a lot of people. What’s happening there? If you don’t mind telling us about it. Well, that was the first conference organized by Cynthia and a few other people, in fact, there was four of us. It was a conference focusing on Deaf Studies from francophone countries. : The central theme was Deaf Studies. It was the very first time such a conference was conducted, and it was a successful event. I can’t quite remember when it was. Cynthia can you help me out? It was in 2015. That’s right, 2015! In Rimouski. What do you mean by francophone? Can you give a few examples of which countries you refer to? France, one French speaking person from Brazil, Belgium as well as Quebec and many others. Yes, from all over the world. Wow! All reunited in one place. The reason the conference was organized was to provide safe space and to gather people together to discuss different issues a nd challenges related to LSQ, LSF and even LSFB. In the past, there were no such opportunities t o congregate, to network and share experiences between researchers from French speaking countries. We noticed there was a gap there, so we decided to host this conference. Daz, Véro Leduc and I organized it. Véro is the first ever French speaking professor in Quebec. And this hearing research director. The four of us hosted the summit. We were quite amazed, because at first, we thought the event would bring a small turnout. We were definitely mistaken. It was actually quite the opposite and there were many attendees from those francophone countries. From all over the world, international. I was astonished.. During this three-day conference, it was an occasion for the different lecturers to share their experience, to greet and meet, discuss and exchange knowledge. It was a tremendous and colossal opportunity to do so and for a first time event, it was quite a success. Everything was essentially in French. While Cynthia and I were working on our Masters, we took the chance to share our work. It truly affected me positively for a first time. And it was the first time there was actual traction gained in relation to LSQ preservation. Daz focused on research on LSQ and I worked on accessibility for Deaf people in LSQ. Normally, with other sign languages, there’s always a critical mass to gather information from. It was not the case with LSQ because is quite a small community. There aren’t so many of us to begin with. In 2015, there was a chance to really get together. Ever since then, research on LSQ and the preservation of it has really taken off. Quite extraordinary I must add. It was an historical and determining event. Well... Go ahead. Go ahead. Finally, it was an occasion to get together and enable knowledge exchange. We’ve been working on this together ever since. I truly treasure the connections I made back then. It truly exceeded your expectations, didn’t it? An inspiration to continue the research on LSQ, sign language and French culture. There’s so many locations in the world where French is prevalent. For example, in French speaking African countries, they use LSF as well as their own dialects. Now, here’s a photo I would like to show you. That is Véro in the photo. In the middle. That was taken when she was still a PhD Student. She’s now a university professor *shows the LSQ sign for university* Oh! even that is the sign for university in LSQ. University. We’re teaching you some LSQ! Yes, we’re taking this opportunity to teach you some. Anyways, we’ve been working together ever since. Do you ever feel that since the inception of the conference, there’s an increase in inspiration and enhanced motivation of the next generation of researchers? Yes. Absolutely yes. It actually serves as a bridge. For example, if I were to do research on something on my own, it would be beneficial to tap into my network to ensure I was on track. There might be an idea I’ve never thought of exploring and with their suggestions, I would look into it. Those connections are essential. Including myself as a researcher and as a member of the Deaf community. Other researchers might have a different perspective on what I’m researching and could help me. That’s why it is indispensable to maintain these contacts. But that’s just me. To this day, we still keep in touch with these researchers. It helps us continue this momentum; you know. Like for example, they could even be role models, that there are wonderful people out there Some of them are even capable of using English. However, when it comes down to it, French is our blood, it’s truly wonderful to use it while staying connected to each other. I know the feeling. When it comes to innate emotions. When together, there’s a boost in knowledge exchange. Yes. Let’s look at the next photo. It looks so cold! That picture was taken in Yellowknife, far North of Canada, in the Northwest Territories. The summit we were talking about, inspired me to continue my studies to my doctoral degree in linguistics. I recently was the recipient of a scholarship, the “Foundation of Pierre E… Elliott. Pierre Elliott Trudeau. Elliott Trudeau. He is the father of the current prime minister of Canada. That endowment foundation was established to support PhD students with their studies in regards of leadership competencies. Last year, I was one of the scholarship recipients. It’s the reason why I went up North to that symposium. I learned from so many people, especially from the indigenous . There is a parallel between indigenous and Deaf cultures. But I felt I learned way more from them. But I felt I learned way more from them. For example, they have their own language. For instance, when it’s their turn to talk, they start their speech in their native language for a brief instant without any interpreters, then switch to English. I stood baffled and fascinated. It was like revealing your essence. For example, I should start my speech without any interpretation. I would tell the interpreters to stop and allow me to sign without simultaneous translation. The audience would then “see me” for who I really am, i nstead of using interpreters as crutches. Reclaim the power and to sever the dependency whenever I want! The tools I learned from the community are marvelous. What struck me the most was how the indigenous spoke about their land and the r elationship they entertain with it. And now, what about the Deaf community? Well, we do not have a “Deaf Country”. There’s no such thing. There is no “Deaf Countries” out there where a passport is required to emigrate to. It is a dream! Instead of linking the community with the land, our home could be found in storytelling and folklore. Woah! Our true belonging to home is found within the stories that are passed down. I come from a Deaf family and I know that storytelling is crucial. If storytelling ceased to exist, then we would no longer have a home anymore. That’s what struck me the most. As long as we preserve stories and traditions, we will always have a home. That realization still gives me goosebumps! Wow! That definitely applies to LSQ as well. Despite the size of the community, there’s still stories within. Yes, yes. Good! I really like that! Ok, so here’s the next picture. What is interesting with the notion of passing stories down is that I can see you are attending many conferences to share and preserve knowledge. Could you share more about that? That one... Go ahead. That picture speaks volumes. Remember when we mentioned the first international conference in 2015? Well, we repeated the experience in 2019. However, is was a more casual collaboration with researchers from Belgium.And their native language, LSFB. There was a partnership between the Belgian researchers and the University of Namur. Though, I noticed something that bothered me a little. It was how the researchers stayed within their own circle and didn’t try to connect or reach out to the surrounding community. It would’ve been beneficial to open up and establish contacts with the community. As they might have expressed genuine interest in learning about their language and attend the conference itself. I conferred with my peers and found a simple solution: set up a Facebook page. That really changed everything, and it ended up being a full house event. Researchers would typically be perceived as eminent, caught up in their ivory tower. Within their own world. But that is not how it should be. It is imperative to include the community. As researchers, we have a social responsibility to share our research topics with the community. During that time, I found it quite valuable to have the opportunity to present and talk about LSQPI. You are familiar with ASLPI, right? Well, we’re in the process of creating LSQ proficiency interview. I was uncertain about the community’s reaction towards LSQPI. It was actually the opposite of my apprehensions. The support was rather abundant. People claimed they’ve never seen such a thing before. An extensive index. I know there are some resources out there for screening, but nothing as formal as this. Never standardized. Yes, It’s like elevating and uplifting LSQ. That’s very valuable to us. It is why it was important to talk about what happened in 2019. Yes, LSQ had already been recognized and formalized, but this time it was with the support from the community. It had made all the difference. Being in partnership with the community was crucial. Yes, it was extraordinary! I am part of a research group at the university studying LSQ exclusively. That research group collaborates with Cynthia’s company, SLCB. Once the partnership was established, things started to take off. With the exchange of our knowledge and competencies, we started building things. With the LSQPI chart, how it would be structured a nd how reliable the testing would be. It shows how valuable and powerful collaborations within the community can be. I also feel that the LSQPI, the real name is EvaLSQ, enables us, researchers, to be able to compare with other countries. For example, England and their “Pro Sign”. They collaborated with Germany and other countries to develop the CEFR, Common Europe Framework of Reference with language. It’s a screening tool with competency levels. It’s really neat! For a long period, spoken languages had those sorts of tools. There’s now something for sign languages. Finally! There’s something for us as well! A special thanks to the Ministry of Education of Ontario who made it possible for us to work on that. And of course, to the university where Daz works and the participation of my company as well. The teamwork between all of us worked well. It seems like the progression of ASLPI in the USA served as an inspiration. It looks like Canada is finally catching up. Was it stressful collaborating information? That is a good question. I feel there’s some advantages about other frameworks already established. We were able to observe what went well and what didn’t and that was quite valuable for us. It was more beneficial than not, in my opinion. Yes, basically learning from other people’s mistakes. Yes, that is correct. Honestly, it’s more like a living document. I wouldn’t want it to be set in stone forever. The most important step is to have something in place. It will inevitably be adjusted and modified over time after extensive testing. Yes, improving gradually. Its essentially part of the science behind it. Yes, language does evolves over time. I’m curious to know, is LSQ based on the ASL model in use everywhere else in Canada? Considering they already have their own proficiency interview. Is there some kind of influence? ASL has had their own systems for a long time. Canadian Cultural Society of the Deaf, CCSD has a screening system already established and CASLI, Canada Association of Sign Language Interpreters, also have their own. However, it’s not yet the case for LSQ. There is still none. That is precisely why we are focusing on the preservation of our own language and how we can protect it. There are protocols that have been explicitly established to ensure LSQ’s protection. I would like to add, that the ASL used in Canada has been inspired by the ASL used in the United States. There is a correlation between those two as well. Did you know that within a language, there is a cultural component? You can’t just replicate a language and expect the culture to follow suit. You must consider the intrinsic cultural aspects and align it with the language. You simply can’t duplicate it and expect it to be the same. : Interestingly enough, there’s some “gray” ambiguous areas in the framework that causes struggles for my sign language linguists and researchers. For example, when assessing an individual’s competency. If you were to be evaluated after a year of barely using sign language. Knowing your aptitudes degrade over time, would you revert back to your original benchmark? How would it be measured? Would it be based on comprehension or expression? Should there be more specialized assessments based on those two components What happens if you have some capabilities in one area but lacking in another? How could one be accurately measured? It is a problem for many experts in this field. There’s so much to discuss but unfortunately time is running out. Sorry. LSQ is concentrated in some precise areas and has been influenced from external forces like ASL. I noted that LSQ is inspired by its French roots and culture. There’s lots of patriotism within LSQ. Is there any confusion from the hearing population about LSQ? There is something called “contact language”. It is found elsewhere with other languages as well, even in spoken languages. It’s normal to have exchanges between languages. Nevertheless, we are highly aware there are discussions within the community on whether we should adopt notions from elsewhere or if we must create our own. Knowing LSQ is a living language, it will continue to evolve. It’s really interesting you ask me that question, because based on history, we’ve always been respectful and on guard towards ASL. The ownership of signs and where they originated from. Is it ASL or is it “purely” LSQ? It does happen sometimes and it’s pretty understandable. There is natural resistance but again like we mentioned students form Deaf schools interacting with each other and the role models from which the signs derived from. The same goes for what happens in the US with the number of students matriculated in Deaf schools in decline. More are becoming day students and it’s essential for the Deaf adults to stand as role models to maintain the transmission of signing. We must recognize that many LSQ signs have been influenced by ASL. Especially for the younger generation. That is exactly the reason why Daz and I, among other researchers, are working so hard on preserving and protecting the integrity of LSQ. It’s a must! Beautifully said, I agree. Perfect, it also relates to the next photo shown here. This is flowing into the next topic on our list, talk about perfect timing! What’s with the green screen? The green screen? Oh! It’s basically a studio used at a Deaf-owned business called Cinéall * show company’s name sign * and TraduSigne * show company’s name sign * Cinéall specializes in video production for ASL and LSQ, while TraduSigne focuses on interpreting and translating services. It also does script translation into sign language. I worked with them on different projects. That truly helps protect LSQ and also, when doing research, to verify and make sure there’s no spillover happening of influence from ASL. Basically, it contributes to maintain LSQ’s purity. We are also developing an app, just like the one in the US. There was a partnership between the developers of the ASL app and the LSQ community. It was great news to hear about the creation of the LSQ app. To see actual video clips rather than illustrations of LSQ signs. Elevating the language because sign languages are actually three-dimensional. Sign language is visual. 3D? No! It is 5D! So, does that mean there weren’t many videos of LSQ in the past? There were very few but now, there is a more extensive collection of videos? Yes, there were little bits and pieces here and there. Shortly after, the number of videos exploded. Back in the 1980’s, it was mostly printed materials, dictionaries if you prefer. You would typically see more of these videos of LSQ, produced by translations from French. Now, we have the opportunity to really open up a nd examine LSQ much closer. Under a microscope and conduct high quality, immersive research. Not much was done back then. In the past few years, there’s been a lot of traction made with LSQ research. Lots of work to do, indeed. Yes! We will be showing another picture soon. From what I gather, it seems like you are all grateful for modern technology, especially during the Covid-19 pandemic. Right? Yes. That screenshot is obviously from a Zoom meeting we had. It’s quite recent. This group permits us to pursue our preservation of LSQ. I think it’s important to describe the group’s participants. First, is Sonia, she’s been a linguist for many, many years. By the way, all are deaf. Followed by Daz, with his doctoral diploma. Then, Alice who is working on her Masters in interpretation at Gallaudet University. And finally, me. Charline. Yes and also Charline. She is the LSQ service coordinator for my company. Before the Covid-19 pandemic, I already had a general idea of how my business was going to be. Providing services for my client’s needs. Such as research, feasibility studies among many more projects. Then I started to think about establishing professional development for the LSQ interpreters like online LSQ courses and even an LSQ assessment system. I already had envisioned all of these ideas mentioned, but happening much later on, in 2021 or so. The pandemic forced me to revise and change my plans. I decided to focus on them now instead of 2021. So, thanks to Covid-19, I was able to invest time and energy on these projects. To really start moving. We’re working on classifiers. Wow! How intense and comprehensive! Wow, LSQ has classifiers?! It has already been two months that we started working on that aspect. It is unbelievable! Deeper down the rabbit hole we go! And remember, we all have different perspectives on this subject. Upon layers of discussion, so many things we have never thought about came up. Thanks to technology. Regardless of where we reside, we were all able to participate in this hearty discussion. Truly wonderful! It’s also important to keep in mind there is regional LSQ. For example, Western Quebec in comparison with Ontario’s LSQ. Often, people would think LSQ being localized only in Montreal, but Quebec city and even New Brunswick need to be factored in. The eastern part of Canada. LSQ is much more widespread than we let on. It is important to keep that in mind when conceiving resources, to include these regions as well. Wow! That’s amazing! We’ll be right back after these messages. We will wrap up our discussion about LSQ. What is LSQ , really? We’ll be right back. It’s a known fact that I’ve been to Canada numerous times, even in Quebec, three years ago. I was presenting an event up there. I did want to learn more about Quebec and I knew it wasn’t quite like the rest of Canada. It was more French than Canada. French cuisine is amazing. Even poutine couldn’t disappoint me. Anyways, let’s get back to the point. I met some individuals who had intense LSQ pride and even some that didn’t know any ASL at all. It was astonishing and it’s hair-raising. I was wondering if you would consider LSQ as an endangered language, under the threat of extinction? Possibly. However, with the recent Covid-19 pandemic, we became embroiled with the Canadian government about the lack of accessibility. We all started contacting different sorts of people. Eventually, we were able to get interpreters to appear on tv during news conferences. Not just one, but two interpreters! ASL and LSQ, respectively! Some people inquired about why there were two. We responded that because those are distinctive languages. They thought sign language is universal, you know? I know, typical! Then Deaf interpreters started to appear. To me, it might not be intended to directly preserve but it slightly helps the preservation of LSQ. Awareness about LSQ then became widespread. LSQ is a legitimate language. Some people expressed interest in learning LSQ. Go right ahead, come on over! In Europe, we would see culture being incorporated with their sign languages. Culture is a part of the whole package, including the country’s own culture incorporated. With LSQ, we have Quebec’s culture intertwined and integrated. I feel that it helps to increase LSQ awareness. The real question remains, post Covid-19, what will things look like? Will there still be support towards LSQ? Especially accessibility, we have to pay close attention to it as that will help us pave the way for the future. Now to wrap up, I’d like to reflect on the period from 2015 until today. I want to acknowledge that many of us worked really hard with LSQ researches. Just thinking of the fact that for the past 20, 30 years, only ten courses needed to be completed to become a certified interpreter. Yes, only ten courses, that’s all! Finally, last fall, the program changed for the better and the number of classes increased from ten to twenty. Now, it’s a university major. Students are also allowed to combine it with minors like math, science or even law. To become a professional LSQ interpreter, that major improvement finally occurred last fall. Of course, we worried about the endangerment of LSQ for a time, mostly due to the size of the population. You are aware there are some issues within the ASL community, right? It is actually much worse because the population size is significantly smaller, lacking the critical mass. Now, with the improved LSQ interpreter’s program, Daz’s research, and Deaf-owned businesses working together collaboratively, it has made the community significantly stronger. Working together as one big family. I strongly believe we will maintain working on and continuing this endeavor. Can’t give up now. We’re not ready yet. We have to persevere. That is correct. On the other hand, we all know you’ve been working hard on this then suddenly the pandemic happened. It reinforced LSQ. Yes! Society would now be confused if LSQ disappeared. People keep forgetting how many Deaf LSQ users there are in Canada. Looking at the French speaking population in Quebec, the number significantly drops when we only examine Deaf LSQ users. I envision and expect people to give the LSQ community support no matter what. Because “it is” the language of the Deaf. Right. I’m glad the pandemic really pushed your projects to materialize. You better not stop working upon completion, I expect to see more in the future! One last thing, what would your last piece of advice be to populations who have similar experience as a small linguistic minority out there in the world? How can support be received from each other? From a linguistic angle, it’s fundamental to respect such differences. Just because some minorities seem the same, it doesn’t mean you should paint all of them the same colour. Embrace and cherish whatever makes them unique. You are such a good linguist. Cynthia? Yes, that is definitely a linguist’s perspective. Now, from a business point of view and as a Deaf person, I think it is important to avail yourself to provide services with your expertise of sign languages. Of course, if you have the proper background or and credentials that is. Do provide services or even hire Deaf people with their own expertise. Just because we are a small population, we shouldn’t think negatively of ourselves. . It can be taught at first, however once you start learning as you go and finding your way, then that will be the start. For example, if I would have my doubts about hiring someone and then be awe-struck how much that person has really grown. I would definitely keep that person to become part of the team as an asset. Also invest in your own people, first and foremost into the youth and the older population. Invest in everyone and work together. Elitism should not exist at all. Absolutely not. Absolutely. That is a must. These people who are getting older, they should pay attention to the younger population. Those in their 20’s or even 10 years old or younger, newborns even. Expose them to language as much as you can. Another thing I noticed, the younger generations are most likely to be plurilingual: as in LSQ, ASL, spoken and written French and English. It seems like anyone who lives in Quebec might have an inherent advantage of becoming plurilingual. From my point of view. Hopefully the other provinces will follow in the footsteps of Quebec and support the preservation of sign languages. Just like Quebec is doing with LSQ. Including America and the rest of the world! Hopefully so! I truly enjoyed listening to your expert knowledge of LSQ. It made this Vlog worthwhile. People should realize how widespread LSQ is in Canada. Thank you, thank you. Thank you. My pleasure! Thank you for joining us on DeafNation Live!

Chapter 20 Helen feels ready for Radcliffe College in most subjects. However, she doesn't feel confident in her math skills. She feels she needs to study more to perfect her math skills. So she focuses on studying math one more year. Mr. Keith, her math tutor, agreed to work with her one more year, so she could feel math-ready for college. Helen felt prepared to conquer the many barriers she would face at Radcliffe. Helen planned to start Radcliffe in the fall of 1900. Helen had imagined college to be a wonderful place where her education would flourish and her days would be full of wonder and excitement. Sadly, that wasn't what happened. The barriers and frustrations were many, and coping was far from easy. Enjoying the process of learning was a luxury lost in the daily struggle to access her education. Helen had no time to contemplate her studies. Other students had a wealth of time in comparison. The pace was set just for them - those who could hear the lecture and read the books. Their days had time left for homework. Not so for Helen. In class, the entire lecture was finger spelled into her hands. She couldn't take lecture notes. 100 percent of her focus was figuring out the lecture. After class, Miss Sullivan would review the lecture, and Helen would type up notes to capture all the important information from lecture. Then she would read the book she had in Braille. Think about what she learned and complete homework. All she hoped to achieve at Radcliffe was hard-won: not easy to obtain. The educational system was not ready for her. Many books weren't yet in Braille. Instructors had no idea how to communicate with her. The large classes were not accessible to her through fingerspelling. No one understood how much more time she had to invest. Sometimes Miss Sullivan couldn't keep up. The rich experience she expected was full of barricades. Helen used a Hammond typewriter which was special made for her. It typed special characters that she need for all her classes, even math. The first semester of her first year at Radcliffe she chose classes she was interested in. She specifically chose subjects she enjoyed. Exams were especially difficult. This was where Helen faced her worst frustration and barriers. The examination process was not readily accessible. For example, her exams were not written in Braille. That meant that Miss Sullivan had to finger spell each exam. Sometimes, a few exams might be in Braille, but they wouldn't be in the standard Braille she knew. Sometimes it would be a Braille she didn't understand. Helen faced numerous road blocks that she had to that she had to find a way around. Helen said that from this experience she learned an life lesson: "the science of patience and knowledge is the most is the most powerful." Which means life is difficult, yes, but what's important is to have the patience to learn and become knowledgeable. Then whatever happens in the future, you will know you can get through it and be successful.

text_english: In her article, Katie Shonk states, 3 Types of Conflict and How to Address Them, there are three kinds of conflicts regarding an organization. There are three: Task Conflict, Relationship Conflict, and Value Conflict. I called them model 1, Model 2, and Model 3 to focus on one kind of conflict at a time. The first one is Task Conflict. Task Conflict is when two workers cannot agree on how an assignment should be done or which direction the organization should go. An example of Task Conflict is when Sandy and Tommy are assigned to change the organization, but they cannot agree on what parts of the organization should be changed.

text_english: Randy Helms, in his presentation, claimed that personality could cause a conflict. Personality conflict occurs when two workers cannot connect, or their personalities are on the opposite spectrum of the personality scale. It is the cause of relationship conflict. The Relationship Conflict is the second kind of Conflict from Katie Shonk's article. Relationship Conflict is when two people cannot work together well on an assignment due to their personality differences or do not feel connected with that person. text_english: According to Randy Helms in his presentation, Conflict Management PowerPoint 2 Script, Malphurs's Model is a four-step process that leads a leader or pastors his teams through Mission, a vision, finding core values, and a strategy that create a new change in the churches. The first step is the development of the Mission. A leader or a pastor will have to create a new mission for the church or organization when there are some changes. A mission determines the organization's direction and helps to see if the organization is functioning, focusing on the organization's future; it guides the decision-process that must make and encourage unity.

text_english: Welcome to Session 3, Part 3. We will talk about three primary ways that people respond to conflict. I think they are essential to know because people should know when other people will react to the conflicts, so they should beware of it. Ken Sande, in his book, The Peacemaker, explained three ways of how people responded to the conflicts, and he called it The Slippery Slope of Conflict . On the left side of the slope that you see in the image, we see escape responses. In the middle, we see peacemaking responses, and on the right, we see attack responses. Let go over the left side of the slope, which is escape responses. Escape responses when people avoid facing the conflict instead of resolving a conflict.
gloss_asl: KEN SANDE, IN X-HE BOOK, PEACEMAKER, EXPLAIN THREE WAY HOW PEOPLE RESPOND TO CONFLICT, AND X-HE CALL X-IT SPLIPPERY SLOPE CONFLICT. IN MIDDLE, X-WE SEE ESCAPE RESPONSE.

text_english: The first one that we see is denial. According to Ken Sande, denial is when a person pretends that a conflict does not exist or denies it. This response only brings short relief and make the matter worse. The second one is the flight. The flight is when someone flees from the conflicts. Ken Sande has put some examples like leaving the house, ending a friendship, quitting a job, filing for divorce, and changing churches. Again, this is not a healthy way to respond to a conflict unless you are full of emotions that needs to calm down before resolving a conflict, or you are in a violent or threatening circumstance that needs to flee away. The third one is suicide. This was when a person lost all the hope to solve a conflict, so they take their own lives. Randy Helms experienced three suicides that took in front of church property. Please get help immediately when you feel like you lose hope to resolve a conflict or anything else that makes you think there is no hope.

Hello, My name is Zihan Wang. Hello, my name is Haokun Yang. Hello, my name is Jingwen. Hello, my name is Yuye Gao Hello, my name is Qiwei Li. Now, let’s talk a bit serious about ICE policy for international students. I will never forget the first week of July, when ICE , who are responsible for immigrants made an announcement. They stated that international students, who take full online courses in the fall semester, can’t stay in the US. I was freaking out. Later, that policy was withdrawn because universities did not agree with that. Now, ICE changed that policy. They stated that international students, who are out of the US and are taking full online courses, cannot come to the US. I feel that is unfair. But since COVID-19 has impacted in 2020, there are also serious tensions between China and the United States. When ICE informed us that controversial policy, I worried a lot. One negative aspect is the time zone difference. I will have to watch morning classes during night time in China. One of few concerns I have with that policy is that I can contract COVID-19 when travelling across countries. Second, I worried that I will lose my student status to study in the US too. I recently moved into a new apartment. If I move out of my apartment too early, I will lose my security deposit. Or I may also lose other things. I don’t want any of my plans messed up. That made me upset. These are the few reasons why I cannot move out of the US. Also there’s no point of benefit if I pay double the price of tuition. We, international students, really want to acquire good quality education. Paying twice the price of tuition is something that we can’t afford. Because of COVID-19, some international students lost jobs. Some that I know no longer worked at Gallaudet due to certain visas not allowing them to work. We struggle to afford ourselves to be able to survive. I am a Chinese graduate student in Gallaudet, as this is my 3rd year. I major in Clinical Mental Health Counseling. I attend Gallaudet, because I believe it includes a strong Deaf community, Deaf culture, environment, and education. As I major in Clinical Mental Health Counseling, I prefer Gallaudet out of all US universities because it offers a Deaf focused program. After I graduate, my goal is to return to China so I can support and develop mental health services. Also, I can provide counseling to Deaf individuals there. First, I am always looking to support myself in the future with work and life. Second, I really want to support the Chinese Deaf community. It is important for me to enjoy and appreciate meeting Deaf people. Interacting with the Deaf is essential to learning Deaf culture, which always makes me keep learning more. First, I want to experience education myself as a Chinese person in American education system to learn the differences. Second, I want to be able to be involved in a diverse community among many countries. This will immensely gain my experience, after seeing and learning their stories. I will graduate in December at the end of fall semester. I will also apply for OPT to look for a job and work experience that is relevant to my major. If I had left early, I would have lost the opportunity of work experience. Not only that, I would miss opportunities to experience Deaf world, like education, interactions, and basically everything. I prefer to stay in the US until I graduate. Also, one more thing. If COVID-19 goes away by next year in May, I would love to stay and relish the experience of walking on a stage for my graduation ceremony to make my memory so precious. So I am curious. Other countries have always mentioned that the US is famous for its human rights. But I feel that international students don’t have any human rights here. Where are our rights? Where do you proclaim for us that. If nothing, what for? Thank you. Thank you for watching. Thank you. Disclaimer: some information varies. It may trigger some of you emotionally, like upset or unfortunate. Individuals sharing their personal stories are without intervention.

text_english: Southeast Conservation Corps is a conservation program In Chattanooga, TN that engages youth in summer opportunities to work and camp outdoors. Starting in 2019 SECC will have an American Sign Language Inclusion program. Similar to SECC’s Youth Conservation Corps model, Deaf and Hard of Hearing teens participating in the ASL Inclusion program will have the opportunity to learn and grow through living outdoors and working on outdoor service projects. Project could include but are not limited to, trail construction and maintenance, habitat restoration, and invasive species management. Each ASL Inclusion Youth crew will camp throughout the entirety of the program and will be managed by experienced staff in the field. Each crew will have two leaders and six members ages 16 - 19. Monday – Friday the crew will focus on completing projects. Saturday and Sunday will consist of recreational and education opportunities throughout the area including opportunities ranging from first aid/CPR training to paddle boarding. Weekends will also be used to fulfill chores for the coming week such as laundry, grocery shopping, etc. All camping equipment and meals will be provided. Transportation from the SECC office throughout the entirety of the program is provided as well. Experience is NOT necessary, just a willingness to commit, push yourself, work as a team, and have fun. Members can expect to gain leadership development, a weekly stipend, and job training. Job training can include anything from soft skills like communication and team work, to technical skills like working with hand tools.

A reading from the holy Gospel, according to John. Glory to You, O Lord. At that time, Jesus revealed himself again to his disciples at the Sea of Tiberias. He revealed himself in this way. Together were Simon Peter, Thomas called Didymus, Nathanael from Cana in Galilee, Zebedee's sons, and two others of his disciples. Simon Peter said to them, "I am going fishing." They said to him, "We also will come with you." So they went out and got into the boat, but that night they caught nothing. When it was already dawn, Jesus was standing on the shore; but the disciples did not realize that it was Jesus. Jesus said to them, "Children, have you caught anything to eat?" They answered him, "No." So he said to them, "Cast the net over the right side of the boat and you will find something." So they cast it, and were not able to pull it in because of the number of fish. So the disciple whom Jesus loved said to Peter, "It is the Lord." When Simon Peter heard that it was the Lord, he tucked in his garment, for he was lightly clad, and jumped into the sea. The other disciples came in the boat, for they were not far from shore, only about a hundred yards, dragging the net with the fish. When they climbed out on shore, they saw a charcoal fire with fish on it and bread. Jesus said to them, "Bring some of the fish you just caught." So Simon Peter went over and dragged the net ashore full of one hundred fifty-three large fish. Even though there were so many, the net was not torn. Jesus said to them, "Come, have breakfast." And none of the disciples dared to ask him, "Who are you?" because they realized it was the Lord. Jesus came over and took the bread and gave it to them, and in like manner the fish. This was now the third time Jesus was revealed to his disciples after being raised from the dead. When they had finished breakfast, Jesus said to Simon Peter, "Simon, son of John, do you love me more than these?" Simon Peter answered him, "Yes, Lord, you know that I love you." Jesus said to him, "Feed my lambs." He then said to Simon Peter a second time, "Simon, son of John, do you love me?" Simon Peter answered him, "Yes, Lord, you know that I love you." Jesus said to him, "Tend my sheep." Jesus said to him the third time, "Simon, son of John, do you love me? Peter was distressed that Jesus had said to him a third time, "Do you love me?" and he said to him, "Lord, you know everything; you know that I love you." Jesus said to him, "Feed my sheep.' Amen, amen, I say to you, when you were younger, you used to dress yourself and go where you wanted; but when you grow old, you will stretch out your hands, and someone else will dress you and lead you where you do not want to go." He said this signifying by what kind of death he would glorify God. And when he had said this, he said to him, "Follow me." The Gospel of the Lord. Praise to You, Lord Jesus Christ.

In recent times during the COVID-19 pandemic, you may have seen an increased use of Deaf interpreters, both Certified Deaf Interpreters and Deaf Interpreters . There are also a few Deaf interpreters who have maintained their older Reverse Skills Certificate certification. State Governor Offices and city mayors have worked to improve access to the diverse community of Deaf, DeafBlind, and Hard of Hearing citizens. This continues in their messaging on Public Service Announcements , document translations, and website messaging. Colleges and universities have also included native interpreters in their Zoom class meetings. Deaf interpreters are unique, as they are competent in two languages, cultures, and are field experts in cultural and communication facilitation. This position makes it ideal for them to work in situations where intrinsic linguistic and cultural differences are present. Such situations might include interpreting for deaf individuals with atypical communication skills; using tactile signing with DeafBlind individuals; platform interpreting where information clarity is essential, especially in cases of informing the public - such as governor’s conferences, emergency situation announcements, etc. Each individual is unique, and all CDIs/DIs bring with them a unique mixture of background, experiences, and knowledge. They all have their own niches and specializations one might be savvy with medical terminology and will take up interpreting the medical field. Another one may have the know-how for platform interpreting with the ability to address audiences of varying language needs and fluency with a clear message. Platform interpreters often incorporate a stage presence that best match the presenter/speaker/official in charge. Every CDI/DI has the skills and the training to provide good service to meet the need and demand. However, not every CDI/DI is a good fit for every aspect of interpreting where one might be needed including platform interpreting, and especially in front of the camera. It is the responsibility of the Deaf interpreters to turn down the job if it is not in their field of expertise, interest, or skill set. The wide range of qualification and expertise for these platform interpreters naturally happens in the field with any setting, not because one is more qualified than the other. This is why we are seeing a wide range of qualification and expertise between the CDIs and DIs we see both in person and on camera. Why am I seeing a Deaf interpreter? You may think, ‘I’m Deaf and I don’t need a Deaf interpreter. I can understand a hearing interpreter fine. So why a deaf interpreter?’ This is far from the case. While a hearing interpreter is certainly beneficial and usually skilled enough to do the job, having a Deaf interpreter has its advantages as they present the message through fluency and fluidity in ASL. Deaf individuals are already native in the language , use ASL features including the face and physical space to indicate ASL grammar that is equivalent with the messages being conveyed. This combination of native sign choices, facial expressions, and use of space and role shifting aligns with the message and conveys a more effective message to a broader audience. This makes it easier on Deaf eyes to see their own language and cultural mannerisms reflected in the interpreting style. What’s the difference between a CDI and a DI? A CDI - or Certified Deaf Interpreter, is a nationally certified interpreter who is deaf. In 1998, the Registry of Interpreters for the Deaf established guidelines for Certified Deaf Interpreters and created a certification program specifically for deaf interpreters. According to the RID website, “Holders of this certification are deaf or hard of hearing and have demonstrated knowledge and understanding of interpreting, deafness, the Deaf community, and Deaf culture." A DI - or Deaf Interpreter, is an interpreter who is Deaf but not certified. While they are not certified, they have the background and experience necessary to interpret. The DI has been trained in the role and ethics of an interpreter but are not certified at the time. However, they have an extensive knowledge and understanding of the Deaf experience and barriers faced by deaf individuals, more so than hearing ASL interpreters. A DI may eventually become certified, and become a CDI in the future. The CDI field is not as ‘new’ as we think. Some interpreters in the field are still working with the previous certification, the Reverse Skills Certificate , which is no longer offered. Why am I seeing a DI and not a CDI? CDIs play a vital part of the interpreting team - when they are available. There are a limited number of deaf interpreters who are certified due to several reasons: During the summer of 2015, the RID board placed a moratorium on CDI testing, halting the CDI journeys of many DIs out there. This may be why you see many DIs that have not yet become certified. Those DIs that you see are in progress of obtaining certification but due to the fact that the RID put performance testing on hold until new tests are available later this summer or fall, they are unable to obtain certification until the tests are ready to take. RID, as the current certifying body for ASL interpreters, has established the Center for Assessment of Sign Language Interpretation to review the examination process and assessment for prospective CDIs. The performance exam portion is currently suspended awaiting changes and improvements. Thus, DIs who want to become certified are stuck waiting for that to happen. So they work as DIs until they can become certified. Also, the CDI field is relatively new, and standards and best practices are constantly changing. We simply do not have a big enough pool of CDIs to meet the demand in the community. In Minnesota, there are currently 11 Certified Deaf Interpreters and 12 Deaf Interpreters who have passed the RID CDI knowledge test and are in line for the CDI performance test. There are more than 15 deaf individuals who did not take or pass CDI knowledge test and do the practice as DI. What does a qualified CDI/DI look like? A well-prepared CDI/DI has expert mastery of languages, both ASL and English, to be able to convey the message sent to a wide audience of various language modalities and education backgrounds. They are capable of interpreting the message through a variety of methods - sign, gestures, reactions through facial expressions; eye gaze; and use of space, etc. This person needs to be able to manage the flow of the information and remain connected with the audience at the same time. A good CDI/DI is able to team with other interpreters including hearing interpreters. The partnership between a CDI/DI team and a hearing interpreter team faces a different kind of challenge of using an additional set of skills to ensure that the message is crafted to best deliver a fluid interpretation while also managing the pace, the content, and the delivery in a controlled manner that is a true cultural match. They also are well-prepared for the assignment with a strong working knowledge of the context and capable of interpreting the spoken message into ASL that is understood by a wide pool of viewers. They interpret the message being delivered without interjecting their opinions or adding information that is not part of the original message. They may however expand on information or jargon that might be unfamiliar to viewers to ensure the message is understood. Not every DI is qualified to become certified in the long run, and not every CDI/DI is a good fit for every interpreting situation. A good CDI/DI will know their limits and stick with their areas of specializations whether it be medical, legal, platform, or video, for instance. The deaf community benefits from having a CDI doing press conferences/live broadcasts. It boosts visibility for the deaf community. It goes a long way with exposing the world is a beautiful part of our diverse world. And that they are ordinary community members and professionals capable of many things. We hope to see more CDIs in the future, and are currently working with a mix of CDIs and highly qualified DIs. So you might see a DI instead of a CDI but we believe that the DIs we work with are qualified, and some are ready to take their certification tests but are unable to do due to the present situation with certifying bodies. Dear white, cis, straight, abled, sighted, Deaf or signing people, Is that you? Listen up! SALATIEL: We are reaching out to you in this video/vlog in ASL about intersectionality. We understand that sometimes our message may not reach you individually or that English text isn't accessible for everybody. We understand that misconceptions are bounded to happen; however this misuse or misunderstanding of this particular term, intersectionality needs to cease now. Stop now. Nah, cut it out. Hold up there. We are fed-up. Enough is enough. "FINISH!" Put an end to it. KAILYN: It is no longer acceptable to us that people are too comfortable misusing it or not exploring the original definition of that term. We are signing this together because... We tired. We're tired! Burn out. So tired. Frequently exhausted. So done. Completely worn-out. REZENET: ...of people hopping on the bandwagon of this hot word of “intersectionality” and re-explaining ourselves or correcting people either based on their academic talks/papers or posts on social media. So, what’s intersectionality? First and foremost, it does not merely mean “multi-identity” which is frequently associated erroneously with this term. There are some variations of a sign for this word: Open-BALL rolling, SOCORRO: Or, . Either one is fine. ASHLEA: Historically, this term was coined by Kimberlé Crenshaw in 1989, to refer the injustice and discrimination experienced by Black women in a legal case. There were some Black female employees being discriminated from two forms of oppression by racism and sexism. So they were systematically set in disparity of power competing with the other greater statuses of white women and Black men respectively. KAILYN: When in the court, the judge told the Black female prosecutors that there was no discriminatory case of two forms of oppressions unless they would pick just one form. They argued that they could not pick just one type of oppression while they were discriminated in both form of racism and sexism. SHAZIA: Now that term is inherited from the intellectual traditions of Black and Brown women, Indigenous women, and Women of Color. ANNA: This term has emerged a new discipline of Intersectionality Studies, focusing on two types or more of oppressions experienced or conflicted each other. It does not merely mean an intersection crossing over of two concepts or beliefs but identities that one lives daily in fear of their own life, stability and security. SALATIEL: Now what? Some scholars in Deaf Studies used a concept that is called "audism." This is one form of systematic oppression that hearing people conduct or believe their superiority to Deaf bodies. To name a few examples, Deaf people may experience unequal treatments from hearing people in terms of being marginalized from any promotion in their occupations, neglected to have interpreting services provided for their accessibility needs, and/or being expected to speak . SHAZIA: However, audism is merely one type of oppression. We ask you to cease from using this alone for any study about intersectionality unless you talk about other oppressed/marginalized identities. If you want to talk about DeafDisabled, DeafBlind, Deaf Muslims, Deaf People of Color, Deaf Queer, Deaf Trans, or others, you will still need discuss about race/racism at the first and foremost since not all individuals experience multiple oppressions uniformly. For instance, ... ANNA: Queer multiracial and multiethnic deaf immigrant from a Southeast Asian deaf family, mainly from an Asian perspective. ASHLEA: Black Deafblind. KAILYN: Deaf Blaxician/AfroLatinx Queer. OCTAVIAN: deaf queer trans sighted multiply disabled neurodivergent person who is White REZENET: I’m Deaf Lesbian of Color and a Daughter of African Immigrants. SHAZIA: Deaf Muslim Pakistani-American woman. SOCORRO: Xicana Lesbian Deaf. SALATIEL: Proud Brown Sordo Queer Disabled! REZENET: Importantly, we do not promote an idea of Oppression Olympics, which is a presumption of a certain intersectional individual experiencing more oppression than another. Please respect each individual for their own intersectional experiences of their multiple oppressed identities. A'ight? SOCORRO: As you realize by now, the "intersectionality" word was meant for multiple oppressions. However if you want to explain that you did experience oppressions but without race, one way is to refer to yourself as experiencing "simultaneity." This term was coined by Barbara Smith in 1983 to refer to individuals who have privilege and/or marginalized statuses. For example, if you experience two types of oppression but as a white person , you still have a privileged status. OCTAVIAN: Now this term is being used more and more in the community and in public discourse. I keep seeing White people lay claim to that term, that concept without unpacking their racism and White privileges, and without acknowledging its origins or how we are claiming that word. Doing all of this without engaging in active anti-racist work. In doing that, we are actively supporting White Supremacy, and making sure it is even more deeply rooted. This exacerbates the oppression of Black Indigenous and Brown People of Color who are themselves Deaf, DeafDisabled, DeafBlind. It is not enough to be neutral or not racist. We must be actively anti-racist. ASHLEA: In closing, if you are not comfortable to discuss race/racism, then it means this word, intersectionality... You should not use it. It’s clearly not for you.

Hello, I am Miko. I am from the Philippines. I just recently arrived here in the US from my home country. I felt safe traveling. I am also required to take COVID-19 test before I head to RIT campus. Anyways, back to the point. I want to talk about ICE. Last month during July, they announced that international students must stay home and cannot come to the US. I was shocked. I felt uneasy. I was anxious, like my brain froze. I was not sure what was the next step to prepare for the fall semester in August. So I want to talk about my stressful experience back in March. During the middle of semester, all physical classes were changed to online when the pandemic started. At that time, I had to go back to the Philippines. My parents were concerned and wanted me to come back for safety and privacy reasons. So I had to follow my parents’ decision. When I arrived back to the Philippines and started to take online classes, I was uncomfortable for various reasons. One of them was internet issues. During Zoom live calls, sometimes the connection would freeze and cause delayed/lagging responses. I had to struggle and bear with that. Second, I also need to ask questions about my classes. When I have ideas for my studies, I need to ask questions. But the issue is the time zone differences. For example, there is a 12 hour difference between the US in the morning and Philippines in the nighttime. It was difficult for me to adjust the different time zones. I tried my best to fit my schedule for these meetings. Third, anytime that there is bad weather such as heavy rain, it can cause the power to go out. I am stuck without any internet. How can I test the internet, when I have no control of bad weather tomorrow? There are unexpected weathers that happen and I cannot control it. These are the one of many problems I faced. The biggest was the time zone difference. It was very unhealthy for me, since it impacted me mentally and emotionally. Despite those experiences, I made it through the online courses in that semester. Plus, the internet problems I faced also impacted with my work performance as well. For example, whenever I work on heavy projects such as filming and photography. It takes a really long time to download my work to MyCourses or Google, watching the loading bar slowly moving to completion. Sometimes, the power would go out and then the upload failed. I have to repeat the process and upload it again. That is a huge waste of my time. That was a very frustrating experience. When ICE announced that they withdrew the policy, I felt very relieved. I was not happy, but I felt that they understood about the situation among Asian international students on how they felt. But right now, I feel happy that I can come back and finish my degree. So these are the stressful experiences I went through in the Philippines, while taking online courses for the past 4-5 months. Thank you! Disclaimer: some information varies. It may trigger some of you emotionally, like upset or unfortunate. Individuals sharing their personal stories are without intervention.

Your Deaf Child, how do you communicate with your Deaf child? You sign, you can use ASL or any other signed languages. You know that it is really important to communicate with your child about safety but sometimes we have concerns about how to start that conversation with you child. What appropriate words what signs? How do you have an appropriate conversation based on your child's age. It can be hard sometimes. Luckily, there is a lot of online examples and different resources you can pick from for your child. I have another example for you, this book. This book is called "I Said No" and it really relates with child sexual abuse and this book is really good because you can start a conversation with you child, you can read this book. You can read this book together, you can have this conversation and you can develop a better idea about safety and the concept of child sexual abuse. Over 90% of people who abuse children typically the child knows who they are maybe it is somebody they trust, some from home or from school. It really depends. It's really important for you to have conversations with your child and these conversations really have to be about concrete examples. Some concrete examples could be like, situations that apply to your child's life and real life scenarios. Like maybe your brother want to play a game with you but it doesn't feel comfortable. In that situation what do you do, what's your best response. It's also really important to talk to you child about boundaries. For example, sometimes people want hugs or people want different kinds of touches that are not comfortable. We have to teach our children how to say, please no thank you. Teach them those boundary concepts, that their body is their body. Some cultures are fine with touch, they like hugs and we need to understand their cultural attitudes. It's really important to teach your child how to say no. Saying no to adults sometimes can be hard. It feels uncomfortable, maybe not sure, it feels like there is a power difference "can I even say no?" We have to teach our children different ways to show that they are not comfortable and gain respect from adults. A really important part of teaching your children about body boundaries is being okay with naming and labeling the different body parts. We really don't want to be confused with secrecy and not talking about our bodies and we really want to know that it is okay, these are a part of your body boundaries and it is also part of body positivity. It will help your children develop body positivity later, knowing that this is my body and being okay with it. An important part to remember to prevent child sexual abuse is to make sure you know the difference between good and bad touch. That really teaches children to respect their body boundaries and respect other peoples body boundaries. For example good touch is part of consent. Your child has to give someone permission to hug them or kiss them. They have to feel comfortable in order for someone to touch the child. It's important to teach your child consent now because later it will help them develop a better understanding about body boundaries, for their own body boundaries as well as other peoples body boundaries. Bad touch is when an older person touches a child, their body parts or when the child touches the older persons body parts. And that is really not a comfortable touch, so it is important that the child understands whats wrong with a bad touch and be able to recognize what a bad touch is. This is not a good touch, and be aware of that. It's really important for a child to be able to talk to you about anything that is uncomfortable, any touches that they experience that are uncomfortable, it's part of their comfort ability level. When your child does talk to you, you want to make sure you don't become angry and that you just listen and you talk to them. This can be confusing for young children because sometimes adults are required to touch, maybe changing clothes or different things. A child might feel confused those kinds of touches, what's acceptable. For example, going to the doctor it is really important that your child knows that that is a different kind of touch when a doctor examines your body, they are making sure you are healthy. Any time your child might feel uncomfortable at the doctor, make sure you listen, you understand and you support them through it. Other ways to teach your child about safe body boundaries is good secret and bad secret. Good secret, for example, is your family is holding a surprise birthday party. The child learns what a secret means but it is a good secret because you get to surprise your family member whose birthday it is. A bad secret means don't tell your parents, don't tell anyone you trust, don't tell anyone what happened and really it is taking away your experience. Now it's time to talk about Deaf culture. It's important to be aware that Deaf people have different ways of expressing themselves. Different ways of saying hello, different ways of getting somebody's attention. For example, if a Deaf person is on the other side of the room, you are going to wave your hands up in the air to get their attention, or you are going to walk over and touch their leg, maybe tap their leg, tap their shoulder. There is a lot more reliance on touch. So it is important to teach your Deaf child that that is okay as a cultural part but also it's really important to know and understand what good and bad touch is. As parents it is really important for you to discuss with your children about body boundaries because you will teach them good communication but also being able to be comfortable talking to you in the future about sexual abuse or uncomfortable touches. Really, communication is the best prevention. Our next video, we will discuss what to do if you suspect sexual abuse.

PRIEST: Hello! Welcome once again to Emmanuel Church of the Deaf, located in Rochester, NY, a parish of St. Monica's Church. I welcome you all here for Mass on this second Sunday of Easter, also known as Divine Mercy Sunday, where we celebrate God’s immense mercy for us. In Mass, there is always an opening hymn which we call the Introit. Today we have a wonderful prayer as our opening hymn - it goes: Like newborn infants, you must long for the pure spiritual milk, that in him you may grow to salvation, Alleluia. I've always loved that old opening hymn, because in ancient Latin, the word for newborn baby is quasimodo. The song is about how - just like a quasimodo, Just like a newborn baby longs for milk, we must long for salvation. Now, you might remember Quasimodo as a character from a book called The Hunchback of Notre Dame. And yes, that story is relevant - it's a wonderful illustration of our point today. There are many people around the world who are like the character of Quasimodo in that story. They’re the people society looks down on. They are the outcasts and the scapegoats. They’re treated like they’re nothings and nobodies, and chased away from every place they try to go. But God loves all of us. And that same God calls and challenges each of us to care for one another - and especially to show acceptance and care for the many different types of Quasimodos in our world today. There’s also two prayer intentions we have for today. Tomorrow is our Deacon Pat Graybill’s 38th anniversary for his ordination to the diaconate. 38 years - congratulations, Pat! DEACON: Thank you. PRIEST: Our second prayer intention is more solemn; we pray for two people we hold dear, Evelyn and Paul Poteralski. May God bless them with healing and interior peace. Now let us pray. The Lord be with you. DEACON: And with your spirit. PRIEST: Before we begin, let us take some time to reflect on the mercy of the Lord. DEACON: Lord, you came for us. Lord, have mercy. ALL: Lord, have mercy. DEACON: Christ, you went to the cross to save us. Christ, have mercy. ALL: Christ, have mercy. DEACON: Lord, everlasting is your mercy for us, Lord, have mercy ALL: Lord, have mercy. PRIEST: May almighty God have mercy on us, forgive us our sins, and bring us to everlasting life. ALL: Amen. PRIEST: Now let us pray. God of everlasting mercy, who in the very recurrence of the paschal feast kindle the faith of the people you have made your own, increase, we pray, the grace you have bestowed, that all may grasp and rightly understand in what font they have been washed, by whose Spirit they have been reborn, by whose Blood they have been redeemed. We ask this through our Christ our Lord, ALL: Amen. LECTOR: Our first reading. A reading from the Acts of the Apostles They devoted themselves to the teaching of the apostles and to the communal life, to the breaking of bread and to the prayers. Awe came upon everyone, and many wonders and signs were done through the apostles. All who believed were together and had all things in ­common; they would sell their property and possessions and divide them among all according to each one’s need. Every day they devoted themselves to meeting together in the temple area and to breaking bread in their homes. They ate their meals with exultation and sincerity of heart, praising God and enjoying favor with all the people. And every day the Lord added to their number those who were being saved. The word of the Lord. ALL: Thanks be to God. LECTOR: Responsorial psalm today: Give thanks to the Lord for he is good, his love is everlasting. This is your response. ALL: Give thanks to the Lord for he is good, his love is everlasting. LECTOR: Let the house of Israel say, His mercy endures forever. Let the house of Aaron say, His mercy endures forever. Let those who fear the Lord say, His mercy endures forever. ALL: Give thanks to the Lord for he is good, his love is everlasting. LECTOR: I was hard pressed and was falling, but the Lord helped me. My strength and my courage is the Lord, and he has been my savior. The joyful shout of victory in the tents of the just. ALL: Give thanks to the Lord for he is good, his love is everlasting. The stone which the builders rejected has become the cornerstone. By the Lord has this been done; it is wonderful in our eyes. This is the day the Lord has made; let us be glad and rejoice in it. ALL: Give thanks to the Lord for he is good, his love is everlasting. LECTOR: The second reading is from the first letter of St. Peter. Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who in his great mercy gave us a new birth to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead, to an inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven for you who by the power of God are safeguarded through faith, to a salvation that is ready to be revealed in the final time. In this you rejoice, although now for a little while you may have to suffer through various trials, so that the genuineness of your faith, more precious than gold that is perishable even though tested by fire, may prove to be for praise, glory, and honor at the revelation of Jesus Christ. Although you have not seen him you love him; even though you do not see him now yet believe in him, you rejoice with an indescribable and glorious joy, as you attain the goal of your faith, the salvation of your souls. The word of the Lord. ALL: Thanks be to God. DEACON: Alleluia, Alleluia! Please join me! ALL: Alleluia, Alleluia! DEACON: You believe in me, Thomas, because you have seen me; blessed are they who have not seen, but still believe. ALL: Alleluia, Alleluia! DEACON: The Lord be with you. ALL: And with your spirit. DEACON: A reading from the Holy Gospel according to John. ALL: Glory to you, O Lord. DEACON: On the evening of that first day of the week, when the doors were locked, where the disciples were, for fear of the Jews, Jesus came and stood in their midst and said to them, Peace be with you. When he had said this, he showed them his hands and his side. The disciples ­rejoiced when they saw the Lord. Jesus said to them again, Peace be with you. As the Father has sent me, so I send you. And when he had said this, he breathed on them and said to them, Receive the Holy Spirit. Whose sins you forgive are forgiven them, and whose sins you retain are retained. Thomas, called Didymus, one of the Twelve, was not with them when Jesus came. So the other disciples said to him, We have seen the Lord. But he said to them, Unless I see the mark of the nails in his hands and put my finger into the nailmarks and put my hand into his side, I will not believe. Now a week later his disciples were again inside and Thomas was with them. Jesus came, although the doors were locked, and stood in their midst and said, Peace be with you. Then he said to Thomas, Put your finger here and see my hands, and bring your hand and put it into my side, and do not be unbelieving, but believe. Thomas answered and said to him, My Lord and my God! Jesus said to him, Have you come to believe because you have seen me? Blessed are those who have not seen and have believed. Now, Jesus did many other signs in the presence of his disciples that are not written in this book. But these are written that you may come to believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that through this belief you may have life in his name. The Gospel of the Lord. ALL: Praise to you, Lord Jesus Christ. PRIEST: I wear a mask today to represent that we’re still living in the midst of a pandemic. COVID-19 has spread across the world. There have been many television ads That show people wearing masks like this In a wide variety of situations. There’s a caption that says - One moment. The caption says: we’re all in this together. We’re all interconnected. Now, sometimes that’s not true; There are aspects of this that are more complicated. For instance, there are people living in poverty who can’t afford to go to the doctor or hospital. What do they do? Some people are trapped in nursing homes without support or help. I recently saw an article about two African-American men who went into a store wearing these masks, just like everyone else, and the police confronted them, thinking they were going to rob the store. There are many people in our world who are defenseless. It’s just like long ago, like in our first reading from the Acts of the Apostles, which was signed by Pat Sullivan, When we are united, that is when we will triumph. I think to myself - what are these passages from the Acts of the Apostles trying to say? These passages are attempts to understand what it means to follow Jesus Christ, who died on the cross in order to save us. What does that mean for us? At the time of these stories, the twelve apostles and the other disciples of Jesus were sad and grieving. They were ill and upset. They had locked themselves in the upper room, blocking out a world that they had come to fear. They didn’t understand what had happened; they couldn’t explain it. Jesus had died - he was gone. And all of a sudden, Jesus appeared to them again - Imagine that! And his first words to them were - Peace be with you. I bet that we are longing today for these words of peace from God, To have his peace wash over us. And the next thing Jesus did was breathe on them. That’s interesting, because nowadays we’re using these masks to protect ourselves from breathing on one other and spreading the virus. Jesus didn’t use a mask - he breathed so that we might receive the Holy Spirit. At the beginning of that Gospel story, when Jesus reappeared, How did the twelve apostles respond? They rejoiced. They were overcome with joy to see Jesus again. Now, later on, Thomas did not believe their story. He told his friends they sounded crazy. So Jesus appeared again. And this transformed Thomas - he cast his doubt aside and worshipped - My Lord and my God! You are here! What a remarkable conversion. Thomas had been filled with the Holy Spirit, and with peace and joy. It seems like the message of Jesus - and Jesus himself - Appear when things are at their worst. Today we celebrate something called Divine Mercy Sunday. That came to be during one of the worst eras of history, At the start of the 20th century when Europe was in ruins from the war. The economy had tanked, there was political division everywhere, and in the middle of this gigantic mess, Jesus appeared to a nun in Poland by the name of Sister Faustina. I found out that her name sign uses the F-handshapes radiating out from the chest, because Jesus appeared to her with rays of light streaming from his heart to show his love and wonderful mercy for all of us. Poland eventually recovered and broke free of Communism, and the chaos in Europe subsided through the mercy of God. Jesus appears to remind us that God is truly here with us. Today, we are dealing with the coronavirus, And it’s a terrible experience of sickness, suffering, and fear - And just like the apostles, we’re hiding in our rooms, doors shut against the world. But Jesus shows up anyway. In today’s Mass, in this video, we tell stories of Christ’s death and resurrection, and his appearances afterwards. Mass also reminds us that the Body and Blood of Christ are still with us and within us now. Most of us cannot physically receive the Eucharist right now. But that isn’t important. Christ is already within us - He’s within us when we love one another, and when we show each other God’s wonderful kindness and mercy. So please, my beloved friends - Don’t forget that God’s mercy is still here surrounding us. Do you believe that? Are you willing to accept that? Amen. Happy Easter, and happy Divine Mercy Sunday. Now I ask that we take a moment to think about the people for whom we are praying. Especially for those people who need help and comfort. Those people who need Jesus to show up. We pray that he will appear here and now. Accept, O Lord, we pray, the oblations of your people and of those you have brought to new birth, that, renewed by confession of your name and by Baptism, they may attain unending happiness. Through Christ our Lord. Amen. The Lord be with you. ALL: And with your spirit. PRIEST: Lift up your hearts. ALL: We lift them up to the Lord. PRIEST: It is right to give thanks to the Lord. At the time he was betrayed and entered willingly into his Passion, he first took the bread and, giving thanks, broke it, and gave it to his disciples, saying: Take this, all of you, and eat of it, for this is my Body, which will be given up for you. In a similar way, when supper had ended, he took the chalice and, once more giving thanks, gave it to his disciples, saying: Take this, all of you, and drink from it, for this is the chalice of my Blood, the Blood of the new and eternal covenant, which will be poured out for you and for many for the forgiveness of sins.
