Humbly we pray that by partaking of the Body and Blood of Christ, we may be gathered into one by the Holy Spirit. Remember, Lord, your Church, spread throughout the world, and bring her to the fullness of charity, together with Francis our Pope and Salvatore our Bishop, and all those who serve your people. Remember also our brothers and sisters who have fallen asleep in the hope of the resurrection and all who have died in your mercy: welcome them into the light of your face. Have mercy on us all, we pray, that with the Blessed Virgin Mary, with blessed Joseph her spouse, with the blessed Apostles and all the Saints who have pleased you throughout the ages, that we may merit to be coheirs to eternal life and may praise and glorify you through your Son, Jesus Christ, our Lord. I am the narrator. I detail what happens in the story. A red-colored shirt represents Jesus. A white-colored shirt represents the disciples of Jesus. A gray-colored shirt represents Judas, who was Jesus's disciple and betrayer. A green-colored shirt represents the high priest, Ceiaphas. A pink-colored shirt represents the scribes, pharisees, chief priests, soldiers and/or elders. A purple-colored shirt represents the Roman governor, Pilate. A brown-colored shirt represents the common people. One of the Twelve, who was called Judas Iscariot, went to the chief priests and said, “What are you willing to give me if I hand him over to you?” They paid him thirty pieces of silver, and from that time on he looked for an opportunity to hand him over. On the first day of the Feast of Unleavened Bread, the disciples approached Jesus and said “Where do you want us to prepare for you to eat the Passover?” He said, “Go into the city to a certain man and tell him, ‘The teacher says, “My appointed time draws near; in your house I shall celebrate the Passover with my disciples.”’” The disciples then did as Jesus had ordered, and prepared the Passover. When it was evening, he reclined at table with the Twelve. And while they were eating, he said, “Amen, I say to you, one of you will betray me.” Deeply distressed at this, they began to say to him one after another, “Surely it is not I, Lord?” He said in reply, “He who has dipped his hand into the dish with me is the one who will betray me. The Son of Man indeed goes, as it is written of him, but woe to that man by whom the Son of Man is betrayed. It would be better for that man if he had never been born.” Then Judas, his betrayer, said in reply, “Surely it is not I, Rabbi?” He answered, “You have said so.” While they were eating, Jesus took bread, said the blessing, broke it, and giving it to his disciples said, “Take and eat; this is my body.” Then he took a cup, gave thanks, and gave it to them, saying, “Drink from it, all of you, for this is my blood of the covenant, which will be shed on behalf of many for the forgiveness of sins. I tell you, from now on I shall not drink this fruit of the vine until the day when I drink it with you new in the kingdom of my Father.” Then, after singing a hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives. Then Jesus said to them, “This night all of you will have your faith in me shaken, for it is written: I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be dispersed; but after I have been raised up, I shall go before you to Galilee.” Peter said to him in reply, “Though all may have their faith in you shaken, mine will never be.” Jesus said to him, “Amen, I say to you, this very night before the crows, you will deny me three times.” Peter said to him, “Even though I should have to die with you, I will not deny you.” And all the disciples spoke likewise. Then Jesus came with them to a place called Gethsemane, and he said to his disciples, “Sit here while I go over there and pray.” He took along Peter and the two sons of Zebedee, and began to feel sorrow and distress. Then he said to them, “My soul is sorrowful even to death. Remain here and keep watch with me.” He advanced a little and fell prostrate in prayer, saying, “My Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass from me; yet, not as I will, but as you will.” When he returned to his disciples he found them asleep. He said to Peter, “So you could not keep watch with me for one hour? Watch and pray that you may not undergo the test. The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak.” Withdrawing a second time, he prayed again, “My Father, if it is not possible that this cup pass without my drinking it, your will be done!” Then he returned once more and found them asleep, for they could not keep their eyes open. He left them and withdrew again and prayed a third time, saying the same thing again. Then he returned to his disciples and said to them, “Are you still sleeping and taking your rest? Behold, the hour is at hand when the Son of Man is to be handed over to sinners. Get up, let us go. Look, my betrayer is at hand.” While he was still speaking, Judas, one of the Twelve, arrived, accompanied by a large crowd, with swords and clubs who had come from the chief priests and the elders of the people. His betrayer had arranged a sign with them, saying, “The man I shall kiss is the one; arrest him.” Immediately he went over to Jesus and said, “Hail, Rabbi!” and he kissed him. Jesus answered him, “Friend, do what you have come for.” Then stepping forward they laid hands on Jesus and arrested him. And behold, one of those who accompanied Jesus put his hand to his sword, drew it, and struck the high priest’s servant, cutting off his ear. Then Jesus said to him, “Put your sword back into its sheath, for all who take the sword will perish by the sword. Do you think that I cannot call upon my Father and he will not provide me at this moment with more than twelve legions of angels? But then how would the Scriptures be fulfilled which say that it must come to pass in this way?” At that hour Jesus said to the crowds, “Have you come out as against a robber, with swords and clubs to seize me? Day after day I sat teaching in the temple area, yet you did not arrest me. But all this has come to pass that the writings of the prophets may be fulfilled.” Then all the disciples left him and fled. Within the temple, there was Chamber of Hewn Stone. It was there that the high priest, along with the Sanhedrin, comprised of scribes and elders, lead a trial against Jesus. Those who had arrested Jesus led him away to Caiaphas the high priest, where the scribes and the elders were assembled. Peter was following him at a distance as far as the high priest’s courtyard, and going inside he sat down with the servants to see the outcome. The chief priests and the entire Sanhedrin, kept trying to obtain false testimony against Jesus in order to put him to death, but they found none, though many false witnesses came forward. Finally two came forward who stated “This man said, ‘I can destroy the temple of God and within three days rebuild it.’” The high priest rose and addressed him, “Have you no answer? What are these men testifying against you?” But Jesus was silent. Then the high priest said to him, “I order you to tell us under oath before the living God whether you are the Christ, the Son of God.” Jesus said to him in reply, “You have said so. But I tell you: From now on you will see ‘the Son of Man seated at the right hand of the Power’ and ‘coming on the clouds of heaven.’” Then the high priest tore his robes and said, “He has blasphemed! Hi. I am Susan Coulter with Deaf and Hard of Hearing Service Center . I want to explain that we got special grant funding for teaching Emergency Preparedness to the Deaf Community. That funding is from CERV - Community Emergency Response Volunteers of Monterey County. We can teach Deaf people all over California, teaching them workshops on how to prepare for emergencies. What kind of emergencies? California has experienced many emergencies. Right now, we have the coronavirus, wildfires, earthquakes, floods, tormenting rains, and more. Are you prepared? What does it mean to be prepared? Being ready includes, for example, having a list ready of your emergency contact people and your medications that you hold. Developing your family escape plan. Suppose your house catches on fire and you all must flee. What is the plan? Your family needs to discuss a plan together and practice. You need to develop a plan for a family meeting place. Suppose, for example, the family is out from home, at work, and at school, and an emergency happens. You can't go home. Where will all your family meet? Together, you all need to create a plan and practice. Suppose you need to evacuate your home. What will you take with you? If you just leave, you will have nothing with you. What do you need to take? How do you plan what to take with you? Suppose you need to shelter in place for one or two weeks. Do you have enough food and water? How do you prepare for this and know what you need? That is the information that will be taught in the Emergency Preparedness workshops. The workshops will be taught through Zoom for Deaf Community. It will be taught in sign language. The workshops are scheduled three times, once a month - September 3rd, October 1st, and November 5th. Those are the first Thursdays of each month. The time is 2 pm to 3:30 pm. In order to join the workshop, you can contact me to get the Zoom ID and password. Also, if that time, Thursday afternoon, does not fit your schedule and you really want to join, contact me and we will work out a plan. My contact information is here. You can call me on VP or email me. Contact me anytime. Thank you.
gloss_asl: X-WE CAN TEACH DEAF PEOPLE ALL OVER CAUSELAND, TEACH X-Y WORKSHOP ON HOW TO PREPARE FOR EMERGENCY. THAT FUNDING BE FROM CERV - COMMUNITY EMERGENCY RESPONSE VOLUNTEER MORREY COUNTRY. X-WE CAN TEACH DEAF PEOPLE ALL OVER CAUSELAND, TEACH X-Y WORKSHOP

text_english: Deaf-Blind, Hard-Of-Hearing,

Hello everyone! Welcome to my channel! If you're new here, my name is Rachel. This is my sign name . This is my ASL Vlog. Hello and welcome back to my channel. So, last week I went to the screening for the CSUN interpreting program It happened last week on Thursday. I'm going to explain a little bit about what happened. Disclaimer, I was required to sign a paper stating that I won't say what they asked or what was actually in the screening. I have to keep it quiet. I can't talk about specific things, but I can tell you the goal of the screening how you can prepare, things like that. The goal of the screening is to test your skills. They're testing your receptive skills and your expressive skills. They are not testing your interpreting skills. You don't need to know how to interpret. It only tests your receptive and expressive skills and your signing fluency. So don't worry about learning interpreting. If you plan on taking the screening, practice you receptive and expressive skills. I have a video explaining how I practiced for the screening. If you want ideas, you can watch that. Really the most important thing is to interact with Deaf people. That's the best way to practice and learn ASL, obviously. Unfortunately now we can't because of corona virus. If you have Deaf friends, you can talk over video, but you can't go to events or get involved right now. If you're applying to the program next year, you have time. You have a year to prepare. When we don't have to stay home anymore, and can go outside again, you should start getting involved. If you haven't done so already. My experience with the screening I feel like I did well. It was hard. I'm just going to say it was hard. I struggled a little bit with some parts. I know there were a few things that I did not do the best on. But I tried. There's also a lot of parts I feel good about. I think I have a chance at getting in. I'm not going to say "well at least I tried" and then give up. I think I could get in. Maybe. We'll see. It wasn't bad. I've been worrying about it and overthinking everything I messed up. I know I did my best. We'll see. They're supposed to let me know the first week of June. Everything is crazy now because of corona virus, so it may end up being later than that. But that's what they said. I will make a video telling you whether or not I get in. I would like to do a video where I open the email and see if I got in, but I'm scared. So, we'll see. If I don't get in, I know I will be crushed. So I don't know if I want to be filming if that happens. We'll see, but I think I'm going to try to do that. Hopefully, I'll get in and it will be fine and I'll be excited. We'll see. We'll see. I'm just waiting for June. Well, that's it. Thank you for watching. If you enjoyed this video please like and subscribe. Comment if you have any questions. Please understand that I may not be able to answer some questions because I'm not allowed to discuss details about the screening. I will try to answer to the best of my ability. So thanks for watching and I will see you later. Bye!
gloss_asl: X-I BE GO TO EXPLAIN DESC-LITTLE BIT ABOUT WHAT HAPPEN. X-I BE REQUIRE TO SIGN PAPER STATE THAT X-I WILL DESC-NOT SAY WHAT X-Y ASK OR WHAT BE DESC-ACTUALLY IN SCREENING. X-I HAVE TO KEEP X-IT DESC-QUIET.

Hi! Today’s tip has to do with gorgeous and colorful falling fall leaves! You may be feeling an urge to rake your yard and dispose of the leaves. This is not recommended. Mother Nature has a way of designing itself to support the ecosystem without human intervention. Falling leaves help root systems, retain water in the soil, and provide nutrients for the soil. When you remove the leaves, you are interfering with the land’s ability to benefit from Mother Nature and its life cycle. It is recommended you leave your leaves where they fall this year and observe how your garden grows next year. Will you see positive results?

anniversary of the international week of deaf people which is celebrated every year during the last full week of September every year the WFD establishes a different overall theme with sub themes highlighted every day of the week it's all designed to unite deaf communities and deaf cultures to celebrate sign languages all over the world and to remind local national and international governments of deaf people's rights and to open up dialogue so deaf citizens are not excluded from government social affairs and schools this year's overall theme is celebrating thriving deaf communities here's the official video from WFD explaining Monday's subtheme cherishing deaf history today is Monday, September 20, 2021 today's theme is cherishing deaf history deaf communities and our sign languages have existed for several centuries today we take the time to cherish the proud histories of deaf people and are thriving communities and the many people deaf coda allies who have worked together to enable the flourishing communities found today around the world there has been a major development in the case of 22-year-old Gabby Petito who has been missing for weeks over the weekend human remains believed to be Petito's were discovered in the Trand Teton national forest in northern Wyoming but an autopsy is scheduled for Tuesday to confirm identity and cause of death now federal and local authorities have launched a massive search at a nature reserve just outside North Port Florida they're looking for Petito's fiance 23-year-old Brian Laundrie who police say is a person of interest in Petito's disappearance but he is not wanted for a crime the FBI is offering a $10,000 reward in the case of 40-year-old Mary Johnson Johnson is a Native American woman who was last seen walking to a friend's home on the Tulalip Indian reservation in Marysville Washington on December 9, 2020 US Interior Secretary Deb Haaland who is the first Native American Cabinet secretary has established a new unit in the Bureau of Indian affairs the bureau will tackle the crisis of missing and murdered native Americans Native Americans are murdered at a rate more than 10 times the national average the FBI is asking anyone with information about Mary Johnson's disappearance to call the number below or head to this website with any tips there is some encouraging news Monday on the fight against the coronavirus vaccine maker Pfizer says its latest trial of the Covid 19 vaccine for children ages 5 to 11 showed it is safe and generated a robust antibody response these are the first such results released for a US Covid 19 vaccine in this age group and the company says it plans to submit to the US food and drug administration for emergency use authorization soon many coronavirus patients recovering from long hospital stays are being dealt another blow sticker shock they are learning that most large insurance companies are no longer waving cost-sharing for Covid 19 care like they did in 2020 that means the majority of Covid patients are now responsible for their full deductibles and co-pays and they're seeing the kind of significant hospital bills typically associated with major health crises like cancer which can amount to tens of thousands of dollars out of the patient's pocket what do you think on October 11 beer maker Samuel Adams is releasing a new limited edition beer that is so potent it will be illegal to sell in 15 states including Georgia where the Sign1News studio is located the new brew will contain 28% alcohol by volume more than five text_english: "Snow-White and Rose-Red" It's an old German Story. It's nice. Long ago, in a forest far away, who lived there? Two sisters and their mother. The first sister's name was Snow-White. What did she look like? She had blonde hair. And she liked to stay at home. The second sister's name was Rose-Red. What did she look like? She had black hair and was beautiful. She liked to play outside. The three of them loved each other. One night, outside it was winter. Snow-White was reading, and Rose-Red was knitting. They were enjoying themselves when, suddenly, they heard something! There was a knock at the door. Mother: "Someone knocked. Rose-Red, go check." Rose: "OK." Rose-Red answered the door and was surprised to see a large bear. Bear: "Whoa, I won't hurt you. I'm cold." "A warm place is what I want. Please?" Mother: "Yes, come in." The bear trudged in and saw the fireplace. The bear was cold and thanked everyone. The mother saw all the snow on the bear's back. Mother: "Fine a broom. Go, go!" Daughters: "OK." The daughters left and returned with a broom to sweep off the snow. The bear felt better with the thick snow off his back. The bear thanked the girls, and the four of them chatted. They also played together, becomeing best friends. The bear stayed there every night until summer. Bear: "Snow-White, Rose-Red…" "…I must go." Rose: "Why?" Bear: "My treasure, I must protect it." "I'll miss you. I'll miss you." Snow: "We'll miss you, too." Sisters: "Good-bye." Bear: "I love you my friends. Good-bye." And with that the bear left. The next day, the sisters were in the forest, taking a walk. Suddenly, up ahead, they saw a dwarf. He was short with a long beard. And it was stuck under a log! Snow: "Hey, look. We should help." Rose: "Yes. We should help." The dwarf noticed the sisters who wanted to help. Sisters: "We want to help." Angry, the dwarf said, "No, no. Go away." Snow: "We should help." Rose: "Yes." Rose-Red pulled out a pair of scissors and cut his beard. The dwarf was no longer stuck, but he was furious. Dwarf: "My beard! You cut my beard. My bear is important." "Dumb girl, dumb girl!" he said before storming off. With that, there was nothing the sister could do but walk along. The next day, both sisters were walking along. Suddenly, they saw the dwarf again. He was near the river fishing. The fishing line and his beard were stuck. Snow: "We should help." Rose: "Yes. We should help." Snow: "Do you need help?" Rose: "We can help." Dwarf: "No, no. Go away." Snow: "We should help." Rose: "Yes." Dwarf: "My beard. You cut my beard agian!" "My fish. It escaped. My fish escaped!" "Dumb girl, dumb girl!" The next day, the girls went for a walk. Suddenly, the saw the dwarf again. And they saw a large bear! Dwarf: "Please, don't eat me. Don't eat me!" The dwarf looked around and saw two girls, the sisters. Dwarf: "Eat them, eat them! Don't eat me, please." The bear didn't listen, and with one swipe, he killed the dwarf. The dwarf was dead. The sisters were horrified and couldn't look. Bear: "Wait! I'm your friend. I'm your friend." Snow: "You're our friend?" Rose: "What?" Bear: "Yes" Suddenly, the bear became a prince. And he looked handsome! The sisters were in awe, but they wanted to know what happened. Prince: "Before, that dwarf was evil." "He stole my treasure, and turned me into a bear." "To become a prince again, I had to kill him." The sisters were relieved and believed their friend. Prince: "I'm happy to be a prince again. I'm happy." Snow: "We're happy, too." Rose: "Yes." The three of them lived happily ever after.

The Communication Access Services program is pleased to announce services for the Deaf, Hard of Hearing, and Speech Impaired statewide in Nevada are being transferred to new community partners starting October 1, 2021. Are you in need of free telecommunications and/or other assistive technology? Please contact University of Nevada, Reno’s equipment distribution program at 833-427-1672 or cas-telecom@unr.edu. They have a new website coming soon. Are you a family with a deaf or hard of hearing child between ages 0 to 21 and need resources or role modeling? Please contact Nevada Hands & Voices at 775-351-1959, info@nvhandsandvoices.org, www.nvhandsandvoices.org. Are you a deaf or hard of hearing adult between ages 22 and up and need help accessing services such as employment, healthcare, education or other social services? A Nevada Care Connection Resource Center can help: https://www.nevadacareconnection.org/contact-us/ Are you deaf and need to learn sign language or need to learn sign language to communicate with deaf or hard of hearing children or adults in your family or workplace? Communication Services for the Deaf will be starting American Sign Language classes February of 2022. If you are interested, please contact cascinfo@adsd.nv.gov or 702-830-9103 to be placed on the wait list and to receive additional resources. We will provide more details about CSD in a future announcement.

Hello! I’m Mariel Ortiz . This VLOG is a part of our new Parenting Series videos This one is entitled “Postpartum Depression.” It refers to experiencing depression after the delivery of a baby. There are many individuals in the Deaf Community who do not know about it. You’re not alone. Most mothers feel embarrassed. They keep it to themselves. They do not want to tell anyone about it. They feel it is inappropriate to experience and that it is hard to express. Most mothers do experience postpartum depression. The most common symptoms are: Crying a lot no matter the time of day or night or crying triggered by the baby’s cry. Irregular sleeping pattern, inability to sleep throughout the night/insomnia, sleeping too much throughout the day or night. Change in appetite, inclination to overeat or tendency not to eat enough. Feeling bothered, worried, anxious or nervous, and/or angry . Loss of energy, lack of motivation, or no interest in prior hobbies or activities. Disinterest in you baby , not interested in taking care of yourself or your baby, or not engaging with friends or family. Feeling guilty or worthless, do not feel like a good person or mother, or hopeless. Difficulty with relating to others, cannot make decisions or focus, or might even feel like wanting to hurt yourself or your baby. Those are some of the symptoms of postpartum depression. Mothers who have and have not previously experienced depression can become susceptible to postpartum depression or prolonged depression well after childbirth. First time mothers, mothers with a family history of mental health issues, mothers with a family history of addiction and/or abuse and mothers who have actually experienced addiction or abuse, mothers who experience complications during childbirth, mothers who have experienced severe sleep deprivation, mothers who do not have enough support or a support system, mothers who already have or eventually develop health problems. mothers who experience high levels of stress, and mothers who experience many of the situations mentioned above could experience postpartum depression. Talk openly with your friends, family members, or your best/closest friends. Take care of your health and share with a doctor or mental health professional. Ask a person you trust, such as a family member or friend, to help you. Make appropriate, not necessarily big, changes in the food you eat and your diet. Eat more healthy food and get some exercise, such as taking walks with your kid, doing some light running, or playing gin the backyard. Be creative! Join a support group with others with similar experiences. Here are three websites for support groups and follow the link that interests you: You can join one or all three of the listed support groups. See a doctor about what you can do for this condition. Contact us here at DHHSC. We offer services to help support you. We will build rapport with you. It is important to get help for these issues because it is very serious. It is very real. This is not something to ignore. It is important to acknowledge and accept. It might only happen once, but it is still important to seek help and get proper treatment. Don’t forget us. We can help you get better. We want you to be healthy and well. It is important for the new role you have for your baby. You are not alone. We are here for you. I’m a mother too. Thank you for watching this vlog. Keep an eye out for our next parenting series vlog. Funded by First 5 of Merced County.

COVID-19 Vaccine— What to Expect. Vaccines are one of the most effective tools to protect your health and prevent disease. Vaccines work with your body's natural defenses so your body will be ready to fight the virus, if you are exposed. Other steps, like washing your hands often, wearing a mask that covers your nose and mouth, and staying at least 6 feet away from other people you don't live with, also help stop the spread of COVID-19. You should be vaccinated even if you already had COVID-19. COVID-19 vaccines can help keep you from getting COVID-19. A COVID-19 vaccine may help keep you from getting seriously ill even if you do get COVID-19. These vaccines do not give you COVID-19. Vaccines are safe. Research shows the vaccines are safe. All the COVID-19 vaccines that are being used have gone through the same safety tests and meet the same standards as any other vaccines produced through the years. Types of COVID-19 Vaccines. Different types of COVID-19 vaccines are available now and more are becoming available. Most of these vaccines are given in two shots, one at a time. The second shot is given between three weeks and a month after the first, depending on which vaccine you got. If you are told you need two shots, it is important to schedule the second shot as close to the recommended time frame as possible. Common side effects. The COVID-19 vaccine may cause side effects in some people, like sore muscles, feeling tired, or mild fever. For most people, these side effects will last no longer than a day or two. Having these types of side effects is a normal sign that your body is building protection against the disease. It does NOT mean that you have COVID-19. If you have questions about your health after your shot, call your doctor, nurse, or clinic. As with any medicine, it is rare but possible to have a serious reaction, such as difficulty breathing. It is very unlikely that this will happen but, if it does, call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room. Getting the Vaccine. Let your doctor know if you need any special accommodations when getting vaccinated. Stay six feet away from others while inside and in line for your vaccine. When you get the COVID-19 vaccine, you and your healthcare worker will both need to wear masks that cover your nose and mouth. Adaptations or alternatives may be available for those who can't wear a mask. You should receive a vaccination card or printout that tells you what COVID-19 vaccine you received, the date you received it, and where you received it. All people who get a COVID-19 vaccine should be monitored on site for a short amount of time after receiving the vaccine. If you need help scheduling your vaccination appointment for your second shot, contact the location that set up your first appointment for assistance. After your vaccine. While experts learn more about how COVID-19 vaccines work in real-world conditions, it will be important for everyone to continue using all the tools available to help stop this pandemic. This means that you will need to keep wearing a mask that covers your nose and mouth, staying at least six feet away from other people you do not live with, avoiding crowds and poorly ventilated spaces, and washing your hands often. Continue following the guidelines from CDC and your health department. For more ASL videos, go to the CDC YouTube channel. For the latest information, visit cdc.gov/coronavirus.

text_english: The sign name for Randy Helms will be RH. In Randy Helm's journal article, he stated that there are three kinds of leadership styles. The first one is the Leadership Style. Leadership style is when a leader motivates people, puts plans into action, and provide direction. Some churches have leadership styles, but most of them are authoritarian styles: the Leader telling other people what to do, how the Leader wants the workers to do his job, and refusing to listen to any feedback from the workers. The last one which I think every church should use and have because it is helpful, and it is about being teamwork. I think the church is about working together to represent Jesus and show Jesus's love and kingdom. The name is Participative Style, which means leaders and workers can decide for the organization, but the Leader still has the final say on a decision.

They have CEOs, under is bosses, under is managers, then under is the workers. A lot of churches, especially denominational churches, have a bureaucratic structure. For example, The catholic church. They have a pope who is the head of the whole church. Under them, there are cardinals is the pope's right-handed men, they work in different departments and they vote for a pope. Under them, there are bishops and archbishops are the people who take charge of their local catholic churches. Under them, there are vicar generals is people who help bishops to govern the local churches. Under them, there are parish priests, and pastors who take care of daily duties like having a service, leading a mass, and hearing confessions. Lastly, under them are monks and nuns who never get married, live together, work together, and spend much time in prayer.

In those early years, MDSC worked on 20 or more major initiatives. Some members worked on getting television stations to expand closed captioning; others worked with hospitals to improve the way they served the deaf community. Other members were dedicated to mentoring younger generations and sharing their experiences. But none of these initiatives had the same life-changing impact as the group’s fight for telecommunications access. In 1980, telecommunications services for deaf people were virtually non-existent. In fact, at the time, there were only a few hundred refurbished TTY machines in the state and little else. This technology was already considered outdated. The Internet, email and social media like Facebook and Twitter were still nearly two decades away. Communications options were very limited. Consistent access to telecommunications technology was essential to opening new opportunities for deaf people. With no effective way to communicate remotely with the outside world and each other, the vast majority of members of the deaf community had to rely on others to assist in communication over the phone instead of being able to communicate for themselves. Deaf people really wanted to be able to be independent in communicating. Because of this, telecommunications access was a high priority. For MDSC, narrowing its request for action was the easiest part of the advocacy process. The need was clear: Deaf citizens wanted consistent telecommunications services and equipment. And they were willing to fight for it.

text_english: Kendell: Hi! I’m Kendell, and this is Brick City News. Eunyoung: And I’m Eunyoung. Welcome back to a another episode of Brick City News, this is definitely different from the studio! Kendell : It really is, it’s a good demonstration of social distancing. Eunyoung: Right! It’s important to remember social distancing means staying about 6 feet away from each other, doing as much as you can online or virtually, and, if you have to go someplace, go at less busy times and be sure to stay 6 feet away from others. Kendell: Exciting news! Denise Hampton is NTID’s new assistant director for Vocational Rehabilitation Services and is now the point of contact for VR-related issues. Denise is also working for RIT's Office of Student Financial Services, OFAS in the next several months as she transitions into her new role. This includes information related to VR support during the current COVID-19 crisis. She also will be able to assist you on communicating with VR services if you choose the alternate grade method of Satisfactory/Pass/No Pass since VR services oftentimes require a GPA for continued support. You can reach out to her with any questions you may have. Eunyoung: The NTID Student & Academic Services Department is offering their services remotely and is available for you. If you have any questions for the main SAS Department, or are not sure who to communicate with, please reach out to Dr. Mary Karol Matchett or Katie Zuris. For Career Exploration Studies, student conduct and First Year Experience, you are encouraged to reach out to Kiersten Blankley with any questions you might have. If you have any questions regarding courses, the new grading policy and things related to academics, please reach out to Vicki Liggera. If you are seeking counseling or psychological services, please reach out to NTID Counseling & Psychological Services. You are also encouraged to contact your individual counselor. For NTID Student Financial Services, reach out to Barb Polle. However, for Financial Aid concerns please contact Alicia Johnson of Office of Financial Aid Service. For VR related issues, reach out to Denise Hampton. If you have questions about Student Life Team, how different organizations may still be reaching out to each other, or any events that may be virtual, reach out to Tim Albert. If you are a student athlete, and you wonder how athletics may be impacted, or you need to figure out if you have any obligations and continued training at home, reach out to Skip Flanagan from the RIT/NTID Athlete Development Program. For Substance & Addiction Intervention Services for the Deaf , reach out to Keven Poore. For concerns regarding Summer Vestibule Program , other orientation programs or activities, or Title IX, please reach out to Amy Stornello. Kendell: If you want more information about COVID-19, the CDC has a variety of ASL videos that are available on YouTube. Additionally, the RIT Student Health Center also has a video that discusses their hours and other helpful COVID-19 information. Eunyoung: Since RIT has been mandated to close its housing facilities to mitigate the spread of the COVID-19 virus, the university is crediting the accounts of students impacted by the closure for the unused portion of their housing and dining services. Students will receive communication from the Office of Student Financial Services outlining the impact on your accounts and the next steps necessary to complete the process. The available refundable amount of these credits will be calculated for each student, taking into consideration all other activity on the student account—both payments and credits. Students then will have the option to request a refund, if applicable, or leave the credit on their student account to be applied toward the 2020-2021 fall semester. For students who wish to receive a cash refund, RIT will process checks or Automated Clearing House payments, if that has been set up, to individual student bank accounts. To expedite receipt of refunds, students are encouraged to sign up for direct deposit in eServices. Students who plan to receive their refund via check should update their address in SIS to ensure that checks are mailed to the appropriate, current residence. To learn more, please visit the RIT Coronavirus website. Kendell: So now that’s a lot of information, and we want to take the time to tell you a few things that might help as we all manage this crisis situation. First, don’t forget to take time for yourself during the day and reach out for support from others. We want to reassure you that RIT/NTID is here to help you, and if you stay in communications with your professor and advisors, this semester will end successfully for you. Second, a great thing to do to get in the mindset of doing coursework or participating in an online class, is to get cleaned up and dressed like you’d be going to class or a meeting. It’s nice to hang out in sweats, but getting dressed and ready each day can help you stay motivated. Third, check your university email and review your new syllabus for each of your courses. Professors have been told to update their syllabi, and as for your email, check it every morning and throughout the day. You may want to set up an email filtering system, so your most important emails are easy to spot. Fourth, this is a great time to learn and practice new positive habits such as time management, organization, or even pick up new hobbies! Eunyoung: I love those tips! I think I’ll review my new syllabi now. I’m Eunyoung, and that’s all for Brick City News! Kendell: Right? These tips are great. I like how my commute is so short in the morning too. It helps me with time management! I’m Kendell, and thanks for watching!

With the current situation relating to the coronavirus outbreak, many of our communications have been moved online to web-based videoconferencing platforms and/or videophone communication. Using those technology channels presents a set of challenges, and as deaf, deafblind, and hard of hearing users, how can we best maximize our use of those tools? Whether participants use ASL, spoken English, Pro-Tactile ASL, or a combination of the above to engage in direct communication between deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing users or connecting through a sign language interpreter via VRS or VRI here are five suggestions that may aid you in best communication practices to get your messages across. Before starting your call, go through a personal checklist. Check your wardrobe, your lighting, your background, framing, and eliminate any possible distractions. This will help reduce eye strain and frustration on both you and the person on the other side of the screen whether it be a deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing peer or a VRS/VRI interpreter. Let’s go through the checklist together. Wardrobe: First of all, are you dressed? Yes? Good. Now, look at your shirt and check to make sure it contrasts with your skin color. If you’re a light-skinned person, wear darker colors. Dark-skinned persons should wear lighter colors. Try to avoid patterns, graphics, or reflective clothes. Think of times where you had a hard time focusing on the person signing because of what they were wearing - did they wear a plaid shirt? A shirt with multicolored graphics on front? A shirt the same color as their hands? Skip over those in favor of plain shirts that will help you and the person watching you on the screen. Now that you’re properly dressed, let’s check your spotlight. After all, you’re the star of this one-person show on screen. Lighting: Turn on the camera on your device and test the lighting. Can you see yourself signing clearly? Is there adequate lighting or do you need to add more light? Too dark? Add more lights. Make sure the light source is in front of you, or slightly off to the side in front. Too bright? Turn some lights off or find a better spot Bright lights will wash you out and make you appear like a white shadow on screen. Avoid sitting in front of a light source such as a window, lamps, ceiling lights, etc., as they will make you appear as a dark shadow on screen. Try not to have light sources in the picture with you as they will produce a halo effect and make it difficult to see you. This is especially true for nighttime calls where there is no natural daylight available. Now that you’re figured out how to shine the light on yourself, let’s check the scene behind you. Background: Look at the screen of yourself. What’s behind you can make or break your ease of communication. While we may want to show off our homes, now is not the best time to do it. Think of your shirt - plain shirts are easier on the eyes, so the same goes for your background as well. Opt for a plain background free of distraction - a wall, the back of a couch, or a portable screen, for instance. If possible, hang up a cloth behind you, and make sure the color contrasts with your skin color. Try not to pick the same color as your shirt or you will appear as a floating head and hands on screen. Now that your background is visible and clear of distractions, let’s check yourself on the screen again and make sure that you’re visible, which brings us to framing. Framing: Look at yourself on the screen. Are you looking at the camera? Can you see your head and upper body? Now - what about your sign space? Are you a projector, signing big as if you were standing on a stage for all to see? Or are you a small signer? Make sure you have ample space to sign, and that the camera doesn’t cut off your sign space. Opt for a comfortable distance from the camera - not too close that you’re sharing your innermost thoughts with the person on the other end, nor too far where the person has to squint to see you properly. Make sure your head and upper body are visible, and that you’re looking directly at the camera and not over or up . Add a bit of empty space around yourself as a buffer zone for those bigger signs so the person watching you can catch every word you are saying. Distractions: While we love to see pets and small children, and know they are inevitable in this time of crisis, now is a good time to make sure they are offscreen and occupied instead of trying to steal the show from you. Seeing them in the background can distract the person on the other end of the call from focusing on you and your message. So shoo the cat off your lap, toss a treat to the dog, place the toddler out of view with something to occupy them, and clear your sign space of distractions The less obstacles in your space, the better and effective the communication will go. Now, here’s a bonus tool - accessibility. Communicating via a screen can be challenging, and group situations even more so. Messages can get lost, and sometimes we miss messages. There are tools built in videoconference platforms such as Zoom, Skype, etc., that can enhance your communication experience. Several platforms have captioning available. When using captioning in group conference situations, be sure to establish ground rules such as only one person can speak at a time. Ask the speaker to speak as clearly as possible. In addition to captioning, use the messaging feature to send important information and/or keywords. For instance, names to spell out, addresses and phone numbers, email addresses, vocabulary, etc., that can be misheard or misspelled by the speaker. This has the added bonus of serving as notes at the end of the call! Now you’ve got five things on your checklist to consider when starting your next videoconference or VRS/VRI call. Make a habit of reminding yourself of the five considerations when starting a videocall or videoconference wardrobe, lighting, background, framing, and distractions and you’re good to go!
